body: v6-dev's accordion is now native `<details name>` (no JavaScript), and the shared `name` attribute makes the browser keep only one item open at a time — enforced synchronously, so rapid toggling can't leave multiple sections expanded. Closing as resolved in v6-dev — please open a new issue if you see multiple open on a native-`<details>` accordion there.

author: ffoodd

body:
This scenario is supposed to fail since `primary` represents a color and you're using the same for background and foreground.

There's no need to allow finer tuning since this use-case does not match how and why our utilities (and color palette) work.

Simply use another utility for one of tour color.
author: ffoodd

body:
Why is it suppose to fail? What if primary color is very dark red? In that case in dark theme, dark red text won't be visible, but background color will be. Having an ability to adjust lightness of primary color would be very useful. 
And using another utility for primary text color is not a solution. What's the point of introducing CSS variables for better customization, if color customization is very limited?
author: Kopyov

body: You're using the same color token for background **and** foreground, what do you expect as a result?
author: ffoodd

body: but that's the point :) Let's say I use $blue-500 for both text and background colors in light theme. In dark theme I would like to make the text a bit lighter by using $blue-300 and keep background with $blue-500. How it is currently implemented - if i change global $primary to $blue-300, I also need to update .text-white and other classes/elements so that their colors are fully inversed. And by changing text color to lighter tint, I can adjust contrast ratio so that it doesn't fail when main colors are inversed. This would be a good example of how it's done on Github. Although the color palette is the same, text color is lighter than border color as they are using different variables (primary accent):
author: Kopyov

body:
I'm sorry but I don't get the point. Utilities  are named after design tokens (in that case, colors). Having primary matching distinct values would totally fail Bootstrap's conventions.

Moreover, whatever the reason are (and for sure using`.bg-primary.text-primary` shouldn't be a thing) one is already able to change color palettes used for each utilities, by [overriding it through the utilities API](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.1/utilities/api/#using-the-api).

We don't support dark themes for now, that could be something to revisit when we do.

For now I really don't see how and why we could consider this. Names matching values is the current purpose of utilities, and your suggestion defeats the whole point.

BTW I really think your example doesn't help. Providing a real use case (and maybe a test case) would have helped to understand your request.

body:
Probably it is the document.body element

You are trying to initialize two different components on the same element
author: GeoSot

body:
If I am not mistaken i'm not trying to initialize two different components on the same element. I'm trying to intialize one tooltip (or more) and one popover (or more) but never on the same element. As in my reduced test case, no element has both attributes `data-bs-toggle="tooltip"` and `data-bs-toggle="popover"`.

I need `document.body` because I need to select all the `data-bs-toggle="tooltip"` and all the `data-bs-toggle="popover"`  in the current page (always on different elements).

The pattern `document.body` + `selector` option is used in [this informative example](https://codepen.io/team/bootstrap/pen/zYBXGwX?editors=1010) provided by Bootstrap (from docs).
author: florianlacreuse

body:
Yes you are right, but unfortunately, this is a big trick.
You firstly try to initialize one instance on document.body, and during initialization, you ask it to be aware of these selectors. 

You may try to initialize them on a different element than body

To be honest it would be redundant if we had figure an idea of document listeners over selectors, but it is something that needs planning as tooltip can be triggered on focus|hover|click|manual and we do not have selectors for this differentiation
author: GeoSot

body:
First of all, thank you for your help. I am pleased that we agree on this tricky issue.

Trying to bypass the check by using different elements than `body` seems to be a hack.

Maybe we shouldn't do any verification / check when using the `selector` option because it's almost impossible to know what's going on? Like this code should be ignored in this case (just a quick idea, I don't know if it's smart):

https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/blob/957c1dd6eaacc7a8b17a68ecb78b3134d449b5c1/js/src/dom/data.js#L22-L28

Moreover, on the other hand, it is possible to fool the verification with two different elements instead of "`document.body`" and two different `selector` options actually targeting the same element.

author: GeoSot

body:
That's the expected behaviour. The doc mentions clicking outside, not disabling `Esc` key.

When navigating with the keyboard, the `Esc` key is the most common and used way to close components, and an actual requirement regarding ARIA design patterns. In other words, the `Esc` key should always close the component.
author: ffoodd

body:
Ok, reading further on the doc I am reading that setting autoClose to false, the dropdown will not be closed by pressing the ESC key. Maybe this sentence conlicts with ARIA design requirements.
Thanks in any case.

<html>
<body>


<!--StartFragment-->

autoClose | boolean \| string | true | Configure the auto close behavior of the dropdown:true - the dropdown will be closed by clicking outside or inside the dropdown menu.**false - the dropdown will be closed by clicking the toggle button and manually calling hide or toggle method. (Also will not be closed by pressing esc key)**'inside' - the dropdown will be closed (only) by clicking inside the dropdown menu.'outside' - the dropdown will be closed (only) by clicking outside the dropdown menu.
-- | -- | -- | --


<!--EndFragment-->
</body>
</html>

body:
That's not something we'll do for at least two reasons:
1. The issue you're experiencing is due to  another styles, not Bootstrap's ones: we won't ship something for everyone to fix something not everyone's using 
2. We have border utilities that shouldn't be overridable, and your suggestion would be able to override them.


A few ideas to help with your situation:
- if you're able to alter `_checkout.scss`, you may try to exclude validation states using `:not(.is-valid)`, or to lower the specifity using `:where()` (but beware of limited browser support).
- if you can't, maybe using [`border-color` utilities](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.1/utilities/borders/#border-color) is possible?
- in last resort, add your own additive CSS layer to override `_checkout.scss` styles.

I hope it helps, I'm closing this as it's definetly not an issue with Bootstrap.

author: mdo

body:
Hi qbus00! I think something I tried earlier could be used as a workaround.  I'm new to Angular and everything related to web development, so I'm aware this might not be the best approach.

I first read your bug report and saw that the recommended "solution" was to opt-out from the uglify optimizations implemented by Angular. I tried it but the size of my project exceeded the 1MB budget so I ended up not doing that and I enabled the optimizations back.

However by checking the terminal output where the raw sizes are shown, I noticed that scripts.js did not increase or reduce it's size while uglify was turned off with Bootstrap JS being the only script. This made me realize that bootstrap.min.js was not taking advantage of any optimizations.

I then thought: _**"What if I copy the contents of the minified file from node_modules, and paste it a line below in the scripts.js file after the app is built?"**_

Well, I did that and it looks like it worked! I deployed my app to GitHub Pages making sure building was skipped, and the collapse animation started working again (visible for mobile only).

> The app is based on [this](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=G0bBLvWXBvc&ab_channel=Fireship) Angular tutorial by Fireship if anyone is wondering, great guide!

Anyways, I hope this helps you and everyone else struggling with this small problem.

author: XhmikosR

body:
As @rarb1t said, by now the only way to make it work is to use a workaround:
Use a shell script to replace angular optimized bootstrap script with original bootstrap minified file and run it after ng build inside dockerfile

**Run script in Dockerfile**
```
...
# keep original bootstrap file
RUN docker/prod/build/copy-bootstrap-js.sh
...
```

**Shell script: copy-bootstrap-js.sh**
```
#!/bin/sh
error() {
    printf '\33[1;37;41mERROR: %s\033[0m\n\n' "$*"
    exit 1
}

bootstrapJsFile=/app/node_modules/bootstrap/dist/js/bootstrap.bundle.min.js;

# check if bootstrapJsFile exists
if [ ! -f "$bootstrapJsFile" ]; then
  error 'ERROR: bootstrap file not found.\n'
fi;

# Search script files and copy orginal bootstrap file 
scripts=`find /app/dist -name 'scripts.*.js'`
for script in $scripts; do
  cp -p "$bootstrapJsFile" "$script";
done;
```

author: yecril71pl

body: Sorry, didn't mean to be unfriendly, I'm just super brief more often then not these days :). Your suggestion of renaming a component class to the name of the project itself, or another vague word, doesn't make sense though. It's a table, and just as say a modal is a `.modal` in Bootstrap, it's named appropriately. We also couldn't rename it until the next major version anyway, if there were a more appropriate name.
author: mdo

body:
> Your suggestion of renaming a component class to the name of the project itself, or another vague word, doesn't make sense though.

1. The *purpose* of this class is to have the table handled by **Bootstrap** (to *opt in*, as you say).  Since you want to opt in to the project, it is quite natural for the class name to include the project name.  The project name is not a vague word, once you have pulled the **Bootstrap** framework in, you know exactly what it is.
2. I have provided an alternative if you prefer to be more purpose-oriented (and to be able to switch **Bootstrap** for another framework without changing the mark-up, which is a pure fantasy of course).  While *responsive* is admittedly a bit vague when taken as a dictionary word, it is a solid technical term in the context of **Bootstrap** (and, more generally, UI design).

> It's a table, and just as say a modal is a `.modal` in Bootstrap, it's named appropriately. We also couldn't rename it until the next major version anyway, if there were a more appropriate name.

The `table` is an element, it is named appropriately by its tag name.  The class `table` does not make a table a table, it makes a table a **Bootstrap** table, hence the class name is wrong.

The situation would be the same if we had `modal class="modal"`.

> couldn't rename it until the next major version anyway

If I had had an emergency, I would have called for emergency service.  Technically it can be named whatever, it can be named `sdf74302wef`, unless I can prove it causes a conflict with another mechanism, which I cannot.  However, it is a pain to the eyes and it is introducing bad habits to coder’s practice, so it would be a good thing to have it fixed at some point in future.
author: yecril71pl

body:
Class name can be whatever, as tout pointed out. So inside a CSS framework, the class name to style table can be table. It makes things obvious: this class is meant for tables.

It's also opt-in since it's a class: you use it or you don't. We cannot target the table  element itself since there are many cases where those styles shouldn't apply: layout tables, tables used as charts, etc.

We cannot change a class name between major versions because it'd break millions of tables in websites using current class names. That's the purpose of semantic versionning.

> However, it is a pain to the eyes and it is introducing bad habits to coder’s practice, so it would be a good thing to have it fixed at some point in future.

Bootstrap's tables are using this class for years, hundred thousands developers used it for years. May we assume so much eyes agree this can be a best practice?

Naming things is hard, but Bootstrap has its naming conventions. They are documented,  and consistent since the very beginning.

And while maintaining such popular framework, consistency is probably the most important thing to care about.
author: ffoodd

body:
> Class name can be whatever, as tout pointed out. So inside a CSS framework, the class name to style table can be table. It makes things obvious: this class is meant for tables.

If it is important that the class has a `table` in its name, it should be named `bootstrap-table`. 

> 
> It's also opt-in since it's a class: you use it or you don't. We cannot target the table element itself since there are many cases where those styles shouldn't apply: layout tables, tables used as charts, etc.

Layout tables are evil, charts are best done as embedded graphic objects.

> 
> We cannot change a class name between major versions because it'd break millions of tables in websites using current class names. That's the purpose of semantic versionning.

That is why I am asking you to provide a less offensive class name that does the same thing; I am not asking you to kill the legacy one because I understand it is impossible.

> > However, it is a pain to the eyes and it is introducing bad habits to coder’s practice, so it would be a good thing to have it fixed at some point in future.
> 
> Bootstrap's tables are using this class for years, hundred thousands developers used it for years. May we assume so much eyes agree this can be a best practice?

No, of course we cannot.  If something is illogical, it is illogical no matter how many people have persuaded themselves that it is normal.

> 
> Naming things is hard, but Bootstrap has its naming conventions. They are documented, and consistent since the very beginning.

Could you point out the convention that says that a class used to mark up **Bootstrap** tables should be named `table`?  Do we also use `h1 class="h1"`?  or `blockquote class="blockquote"`?

> 
> And while maintaining such popular framework, consistency is probably the most important thing to care about.

:+1: of course.

Bottom line though is that we're not going to be renaming the class :). There's no better name for a table than table in this situation. There's the possibility of prefixing classes, but I don't see much of a need there when there are also tools that can help with that.

author: ffoodd

body: I've previously been against using extends, largely because they cannot be used in media queries if declared outside the media context. See https://sass-lang.com/documentation/at-rules/extend#extend-in-media for details. Beyond that, it creates additional selectors quite quickly, which I've considered a complication and performance risk when working at scale.
author: mdo

body:
Yes, extending from classes gets pretty bad quickly. I'd love to not use `@extend`, however I don't see a different solution on how to apply our consistent Bootstrap styling to components where we don't control the HTML (JS manipulation isn't an option, obviously 😄).

Extending from silent classes (or "placeholders" was also a term I found the `%` selectors) will just apply the styling inside - avoiding bloated selectors you get when extending from classes. That's why I want to introduce silent classes as well, at the same place where the class styling is defined.
author: saitho

body: So, is it possible to move forward with the PR #35863, or is silent classes something you definitely won't add to Bootstrap?
author: saitho

body:
The discussion is opened, there're quite a few questions to solve and such feature takes time.

If we go with this, why not defining silent classes for other components, too? What about utilities?

For now I'm not convinced at all. First of all, it's not something we'd be able to maintain IMHO, because tons of situations wouldn't work for other components (and why would we only use this for buttons only?).

Moreover regarding your use case, that's not an issue with Bootstrap. You're able to add those classes with JavaScript, or to make a custom CSS build to add your selectors.

So either we fully go with silent classes usages, meaning it should work for any components and be documented as a new kind of usage, or we don't land this and maybe add something handling your use case in our docs (upcoming snippets, maybe?).

Still open for discussion but that's not so simple.
author: ffoodd

body:
> You're able to add those classes with JavaScript, or to make a custom CSS build to add your selectors.

I'm not a fan of DOM manipulation just to add classes - especially if the HTML that we need to modify is also added via JavaScript. We're already doing a custom CSS build with Bootstrap SCSS files. So we might just add the silent classes via patch where we need them for now.

I was just wondering if that may also be an issue for other developers – so this could be done directly in Bootstrap without the need to patch.
author: saitho

body:
Honestly I don't know, but AFAIK this hasn't been requested before—but I didn't check.

Moving to this would be a tough work. In your case, workarounds exist and the issue is not with Bootstrap but with another script adding markup roughly.

Bootstrap is meant to apply on specific markup, handling other markup is out scope. And I'm really concerned about what could come next, if we try to take such cases into account.
author: ffoodd

body:
I was seeking a way to use `@extend` with placeholders as well (I don't think `@extend` should be used directly on a class).
I have a use-case of wanting to apply `text-truncate` styles to an existing class.
It is safer for me to extend the Bootstrap utility, knowing that if something is fixed in the future regarding how to handle text ellipsis, the fix will be applied to my code as well.

That's only one example but I am sure everyone would find something useful.
author: jleroy-dev
saitho

body:
My issue is with components. I agree adding silent classes for components gets pretty complex as nesting and different states happen.

For now I patched the SCSS to add the silent classes I need and was able to reduce my dev CSS by 50%. :+1: 
Maybe we can add some documentation on the issues with `@extend` and how to deal with them properly?

Such may include:
- avoid them in genreral by applying Bootstrap classes to components (e.g. via JS)
- only extending from Bootstrap utility classes AND not modifying Bootstrap utility classes in nested contexts
- only extending from silent classes which one needs to patch into the Bootstrap package e.g. via [patch-package](https://www.npmjs.com/package/patch-package) – possibly increasing the maintainance during upgrades of Bootstrap in the own project

Closing for now and labelling for v6 to revisit for then.

body:
1. Checks, radios, and switches are sized with font-size. Annoying in some respects, but also a nice convenience IMO. We'll stick with that for now.
2. Good callout, I've opened #36156 to address that.
3. I see what you're going for, but using we'll stick to explicit `width` dimensions for sizing inputs. `minlength` and `maxlength` affect the number of characters within an input, not it's size. `size` is interesting, but going by character count when variable fonts across browsers and devices is a recipe for some difficult inconsistencies.
4. No plans to add form-wide sizing.

body: The modal code tries to maintain focus inside the modal once opened. It seems CKE creates its own little popup but inserts it dynamically outside of the container for the modal. Unfortunately, I don't think that's something that can be fixed at Bootstrap's level (as it needs to enforce its focus to remain inside the modal itself), and it's specific to what CKE does in how it generates its extra tooltips etc - it should ideally do them as sibling elements of its regular UI (which *is* inside the modal).
author: patrickhlauke

body:
@patrickhlauke : Thanks for explanation, but...

> extra tooltips etc - it should ideally do them as sibling elements of its regular UI 

Not even bootstrap does that with tooltips. Even modals and popovers are recommended to be placed at the end of `<body>`, because the CSS can easily be broken, e.g. when placed in navs, etc.

> The modal code tries to maintain focus inside the modal once opened

The documentation doesn't mention it. It says only "Puts the focus on the modal when initialized.". 
Maybe the focus option could have another value, like "init"?
author: Liero

body:
This issue still exists in 5.3.3. Are there any plans to fix this?

Setting the `focus` option to false has the side effect of not being able to close the modal with the `ESC` key.
The documentation still states that the option `Puts the focus on the modal when initialized.`. It would have been fine if it was doing it only on initialize - why does it need to steal the focus constantly?
author: pmishev

body: it doesn't "steal" the focus, it maintains it inside the modal dialog, which is what it should do to be a conformant accessible modal dialog. the core issue here are components that, when inside a dialog, still assume they can generate their containers on the fly and just put them at the end of the DOM

author: tguimmaraess

body:
I don't know if I have the exact same issue, but it feels similar. I'm using Vue to toggle the "closable" state of the modal to prevent users from closing the modal while some async operation is ongoing.

I originally tried to recreate the modal every time the state changes (with some workaround to prevent the animation). That caused the modal to not fully close due to some object references gone wrong I assume. The `_backdrop._element` was no longer on the DOM while an equal object was (but BS had no reference to it).

I fixed my "toggle closable" using this:

```js
this.bsModal._backdrop.isVisible = Boolean(this.closable ? true : 'static')
this.bsModal._config.keyboard = this.keyboard && this.closable
``` 

Maybe this will help some of you

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/895831/181859232-3b9c5e9c-c9f3-4f34-ba50-3b69b8302d6c.png)


## Tooltips

* Arrow looks awkward/wrong
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/895831/181859223-212bf331-3f60-4c8e-aa23-c0559b731199.png)

# Other

A few other examples not covered in the cheatsheet:

* toggle buttons https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.2/components/buttons/#toggle-states don't visually differentiate active one
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/895831/181859558-46279dea-476b-467a-9dea-f6379f4fe5bc.png)

* checkbox/radio button group https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.2/components/button-group/#checkbox-and-radio-button-groups don't differentiate checked/unchecked

*  as already seen in the alert and modal above, close button https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.2/components/close-button/ doesn't show at all (except for its white variant)

body:
Another thing I didn't see in previous comments is the fact that you can't see the `checked` state of the checkboxes when viewed in certain themes, such as High Contrast White in Windows 10 and  Desert High Contrast in Windows 11.  You can find an explanation of the issues I found with this control and my proposed solutions in this CodePen:
[Fixing Accessibility of Bootstrap's Checkbox Styling](https://codepen.io/rfentress/pen/jOpEbYa)

Here is a gist of the same, basically: [Contrast tweaks for checkbox in Bootstrap](https://gist.github.com/robfentress/c77de59002fc07fbd6d802eac0b1f2f0)

This is easy to miss, because it doesn't show up when checking with darker themes, which is all most people test with.

I'm not sure, but I think I remember there being similar issues with the radio buttons.
author: robfentress

body:
I spent a lot of time going through the components and have created a `forced-colors: active` stylesheet which fixes just about all issues I could find in both dark and light high contrast themes. Tested with Edge on Windows 11.

- GitHub repo: https://github.com/coliff/bootstrap-forced-colors-css
- npm: https://www.npmjs.com/package/bootstrap-forced-colors-css
- Demo: https://coliff.github.io/bootstrap-forced-colors-css/tests/

Some fixes made are a bit opinionated, e.g. increasing border width from 1px to 2px on form inputs to counter the fact that there is no box-shadow. It'd be great to see some/most of these fixes be made to Bootstrap in the future. For now though, users can use this stylesheet to improve things for Windows users with High Contrast themes.

```html
<link
  rel="stylesheet"
  href="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/bootstrap-forced-colors-css@1/css/bootstrap-forced-colors.min.css" 
  media="screen and (forced-colors: active)">
```

<details>
<summary>Testing with Forced Colors Mode Emulation in Chrome / Edge</summary>

![edge-test-forced-colors-active](https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/assets/1212885/dc681871-7605-42d8-82d0-b6361e7f3faa)

![edge-test-forced-colors-active-light-mode](https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/assets/1212885/df7766a8-d825-4845-8a2e-1f733dbd38e8)

</details>

author: GeoSot

body: to add to this: while the html spec removed restrictions on `id`, allowing any kind of string (as long as it has at least one character, and doesn't contain spaces), CSS3 still disallows `id` values that start with a number. and as `document.querySelector` effectively runs a CSS query, any `id`s that start with a number will fail.

Select menus don't have `placeholder`s, they show their first `option`. You're using a `.form-select` class on an input, which is incorrect. This also isn't a breaking change since the v4 version was just an example and not an officially supported component.

body:
We do have our own `color-contrast()` function that could be used everywhere to enforce contrasts—which has been suggested through a PR last year (#34045) and closed because of its too many impacts.

There's no need for another mixin or function since we have a working solution. If we move to enforcing contrasts, we'll simply use our existing function and declare both foreground and background colors altogether (which is the whole point, after all).

BTW since the intention is to change appearance of some components, so it's necessarily a BC. As you explained, projects would need to override some colors to cancel the change: that's breaking.

I'm closing this for now, but will keep an eye on the topic since I'd really like to ensure contrasts everywhere, toi!

body:
## Describe the issue I stumbled upon this when dealing with the Collapse plugin. The collapse event handlers, too, are added with `useCapture = true`. For me this causes problems for buttons in card headers. In my case clicking the card header collapses card body, and there is no way for me to prevent clicks on the button from toggling the card. ## Reduced test case ```html

Card Title

Collapsed content
``` https://jsfiddle.net/gehxa4s5/ ## What operating system(s) are you seeing the problem on? Windows 10 ## What browser(s) are you seeing the problem on? Chrome 108.0.5359.125 (Official Build) (64-Bit) ## What version of Bootstrap are you using? 5.2
author: mwaibel-go

body:
I'm running into exactly this same thing when migrating from Bootstrap 4 to 5, also in the context of trying to make sure that clicking buttons in a collapsible row doesn't also collapse it.

I spent some time looking into this. There's at least one somewhat valid use case for handling events in the capture phase: the `Esc` key in dropdowns and such. Because those events are handled at the document level via event delegation, there's a desire to avoid `Esc` bubbling to parent elements. I assume this is for things like dropdowns inside of modals.

However, I'm inclined to say that things like that should be the exception, not the rule. That is: default to handle during bubbling, but judiciously use the capture phase when it's specifically necessary.

body:
Some tests done with the [current 5.1 version](https://getbootstrap.com) and the [current _main_ version](https://twbs-bootstrap.netlify.app).

Before, I can observe this rule:
1. If the component already has a border by design, using `.border-{color}` will change the color ([alerts](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.1/components/alerts/), [buttons](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.1/components/buttons/), `<input>`, etc.) 
2. If the compoent doesn't already have a border by design, using `.border-{color}` is not enough and need `.border` as well ([badge](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.1/components/badge/#headings), `<span>`, [close button](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.1/components/close-button/#example), etc.)
In the [border color utility documentation](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.1/utilities/borders/#border-color) we have both `.border` and `.border-{color}` because the "component" is a simple `<span>`.

Now, in the _main_ branch:
1. All "basic components" that have already a border by design like `<input>`s need an extra `.border`.
2. All components already migrated with CSS vars need an extra `.border` as well.

Indeed the alerts for example are defined like this: `border: var(--bs-alert-border)` with `--bs-alert-border: 1px solid var(--bs-alert-border-color);` with `--bs-alert-border-color: #b6d4fe` (for example).
Adding a `.border-danger` to this alert will do `--bs-border-color: rgba(var(--bs-danger-rgb), var(--bs-border-opacity));` which won't override the border color since the border will still use the `--bs-alert-border-color`. To override this behavior, `.border` must be added as well.

**Note:** We don't have the same issue with `.bg-{color}` because it will do for example `background-color: rgba(var(--bs-danger-rgb),var(--bs-bg-opacity)) !important;`. **I haven't tested it** so it is maybe something stupid but maybe we could do the same with borders. Instead of just changing the CSS var do something like:
```scss
.border-danger {
   border-color: var(--bs-danger-rgb) !important;
}
```

body:
We didn't even start working on V6 since we're only getting feedback and moving on v5.

`aspect-ratio` property as a utility can already be achieved [thanks to the utilities API](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.1/utilities/api/#add-utilities), and even made responsive. For people coming across this issue, and   whose [browser support allow to use this property](https://caniuse.com/mdn-css_properties_aspect-ratio), this is really easy:

```scss
$utilities: map-merge(
  $utilities,
  (
    "aspect-ratio": (
      property: aspect-ratio,
      responsive: true,
      values: '1', '4 / 3', '16 / 9'
    )
  )
);
```

Done!

body:
Hello there! 1. First, notice you imported the compiled bundle of Bootstrap. Then, you want to use the ESM version of bootstrap in the same project. This confuses the module bundler of Create React App which is Webpack. 2. Read about using Bootstrap with Webpack module bundler (included in Create React App) [here](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.1/getting-started/webpack/) 3. I got the same issue with collapse (in the navbar component) and tooltips using React. I solved in the following way: 3.1 Importing only the CSS file in the index.js file or the App.js file and not importing the bundle JS file. ```js // ... import "bootstrap/dist/css/bootstrap.min.css"; // ... ``` 3.2 I am using the collapse feature in my navbar component. I am not importing bootstrap in this file (Navbar.jsx) only configuring the HTML attributes, like the following code: ```html
``` 3.3 Then, I am using the Tooltip feature like this (here I am importing bootstrap ESM and the Webpack module bundler bundles correctly): ```js // ... import { Tooltip } from "bootstrap"; // ... const enableTooltip = (someHtmlElement) => { new Tooltip(someHtmlElement); }; // ... ``` I used this to make the Collapse feature and the tooltip component work correctly together in the same project. You can try this way to use Bootstrap + React. Additional Notes: - Bootstrap v5.2.0 - React v18.2.0 - React Scripts: v5.0.1 - Notice in your "node_modules" folder that PopperJS (Bootstrap's peer dependency) is installed when you install bootstrap.

body:
Hi,
I had the same issue migrating a project to bootstrap 5 (from bootstrap 4) and Angular v11 to v15.

My setup wrong (but working with v4 somehow. It was like that was:

angular.json
```
"styles": [
  "src/styles.scss"
],
```
src/styles.scss
```
@import './variables.scss';
```
src/variables.scss
```
@import 'bootstrap'; 
```

In the variables.scss I had some variables that I import and use in the scss files of some components.
I think that importing multiple times bootstrap (indirectly via the variable.scss file) broke the css.

To solve that, I've simply moved the `@import 'bootstrap'; ` in the `src/styles.scss` file (that I load only once).

I hope it can help people struggling on the same issue.

body:
Unfortunately we're a little limited in what we can do here. We mention this in our docs: https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.1/customize/overview/#csps-and-embedded-svgs.

Your best option is replacing the element. Note that we don't do this ourselves for a few reasons currently:

- It'd be a breaking change in v5.
- It's not possible in every situation (e.g., `background-image`s on inputs for form validation)
- It'd mean including additional files in our `dist` builds for all our icons

Sorry for the bad news. Always open to new ideas here, so if there's more we can do—maybe we include more code snippets in our docs?—please let us know.

body:
**This most certainly didn't work for me.**
> npm install autoprefixer@10.4.5 --save-exact

**But following helped remove the warning.**

In the package.json of the top level directory of the react app, add the following section after the ```dependencies: {..}```
```
"overrides": {
    "autoprefixer": "10.4.5"
  }
```

- delete package-lock.json
- \rm -rf node_modules
- npm install
- restart the react app with "react-scripts start"

The warning is no more. For more information about "dependencies", see following


https://www.stefanjudis.com/today-i-learned/how-to-override-your-dependencys-dependencies/

author: shehi

body:
After many tries, only this worked for me:

- Install force-resolutions
`npm install force-resolutions --save-dev`
- Add preinstall to your scripts on package.json: 
`"preinstall": "npx force-resolutions"`
- Add resolutions to your package.json
```
"resolutions": {
      "autoprefixer": "10.4.5"
},
```
- Clean your node_modules and package-lock
```
rm -rf node_modules
rm -rf package-lock.json
```
- Install again
`npm install`

- Check your package-lock and see if correct versions were installed.
- Make sure to have package-lock pushed to your repository.

author: Mona1014

body:
To fix this issue for bootstrap 4.6.1 users who cannot or do not want to upgrade yet to bootstrap beta 5.2.0 I managed to fix the issue by doing the following:

- Delete package-lock.json file
- Delete node_modules folder
- Set "autoprefixer": "10.4.5" in your dependencies (add it when it is not there)
- Set "postcss-preset-env": "7.0.0" in your dependencies (add it when it is not there)
- Run npm install

The postcss-preset-env package uses autoprefixer to validate the bootstrap css. With a clean install postcss-preset-env was set to 7.0.1 for my application. I am not sure missing out on fixes from 7.0.0 to 7.0.1 will cause other issues, but my application ran fine with this setup.
author: jobwttraining

body:
Stop promoting downgrading dependencies as a fix or solution. It is not. You don't fix the problem of using something that is deprecated, you are just suppressing a warning and ignoring it.

There is already a PR for fixing the warning in 4.7: https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/pull/36283
But it looks like there isn't an agreement about it.
author: mmarton

body:
Sorry mate, but breaking changes like this one in autoprefixer should not be introduced in a minor/patch version, but should always be a major version. This gives everybody the time to upgrade their dependecies accordingly. npm run build now fails because of this warning halting production on our CI/CD servers. We cannot wait untill 4.7 is released to fix this issue.

Can you please explain how we can suppress and ignore only this warning? We want clean code, so we are not going to disable warnings for npm run build completely.

author: shehi

body:
@[jobwttraining](https://github.com/jobwttraining) 
I wasn't targeting you in my comment, just wanted to state, that ignoring a problem is not a solution (maybe a tmp patch)

A deprecation is exactly the type of thing that happens in minor releases before the new major completely remove support for that functionality and introduces a real breaking change.

This is a complex issue
- color-adjust is a deprecated (https://www.w3.org/TR/css-color-adjust-1/#propdef-color-adjust) and not well supported (https://caniuse.com/?search=color-adjust) css property
- autoprefixer deprecated it's support too, to remove it eventually
- there shoud be a difference between deprecation warnings and other usage warnings, but there isn't

something similar happened when sass deprecated the division with "/". The "solution" was then a switch to sass or sass-loader to to be able to ignore warnings from node-modules and eventually bootstrap released their fix.

If you use webpack you can try to suppress only that message/file (still better than being on outdated libs) https://webpack.js.org/configuration/other-options/#ignorewarnings (I didn't try this, it's just a possibility)

The real solution should come from bootstrap by merging https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/pull/36283

author: mdo

body: Sorry about the title. My real-world scenario is more a combination of a smaller field (on mobile) and a somewhat longer label: In a registration form you can enter a name prefix like "Dr." and also a name suffix like "MBA". These fields are typically not very large as the input value is most likely short too. In German one would use labels like "Titel vorangestellt" and "Titel nachgestellt". So not super long, but can be a bit longer than the field on mobile, see screenshots attached. When cutting these labels you still get what the input value ought to be and avoid kind of a UI bug ...

body:
Here is an example, using Jquery, of the URL-change-on-scroll feature I am suggesting for bootstrap's js library so that Jquery is not required: http://scrollsample.appspot.com/items The demo divides the sections into "pages". As you scroll down to the next page, the URL is appended with the page name. However, at the top, Page 1, it doesn't do so since the base URL already defaults to the top. It appears the URL is changed to the name given in the data-page-url attribute defined in each div demarking each page. However, I suggest that the URL be appended with the id anchor name so that the behaviour is the same as if the user clicked to the section via the menu, to avoid extra coding. For example, scrolling to the third section of a page would change https://genericsite.com/ to https://genericsite.com/#section-3, the id associated with `Section 3` and `
`.

> Many pages disable the underline on links and have link highlighting only by color.

As a general rule, this will introduce accessibility issues to your website. It violates WCAG 2.1 Level A.

> 1.4.1 Use of Color: Color is not used as the only visual means of conveying information, indicating an action, prompting a response, or distinguishing a visual element.

https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG20-TECHS/F73.html

author: WinterSilence

body: Sorry, I knew I should've been more specific. There's been no _functional_ change to the headings in v5.2.0-beta1. Heading color has always been a `null` value, and now so is `color: var(--bs-heading-color)`. Look at `:root` and you'll find `--bs-heading-color: ;`. There's no new color being set here, despite the addition of the CSS variable. In v5.2.0-beta1, now with CSS variable: https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/blob/fca04c07131a81e625a516cf7b98a8c7df0df1c1/scss/_reboot.scss#L83-L91 And in the browser, you can see that it takes no effect because the variable is empty: In v5.1.3, with the Sass variable (also set to `null` in `_variables.scss`): https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/blob/1a6fdfae6be09b09eaced8f0e442ca6f7680a61e/scss/_reboot.scss#L88-L96 Sass takes a property with `null` value and removes it from the compiled CSS. But that doesn't happen with CSS variables, they just get rendered out there. We could perhaps do a null CSS variable check and remove the property entirely, but that prevents the use case of CSS variables entirely.

body: The documentation (https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.2/components/scrollspy/) is not clear. It states that smooth scroll "enables smooth scrolling when a user clicks on a link that refers to ScrollSpy observables." The term "smooth scrolling" usually refers to the animated smooth scrolling effect when clicking on a link to a different part of the page. For an example, see https://codepen.io/prvnbist/pen/GQMPZq  click on a  top right menu item and see how the page slowly scrolls (animates scrolling) to the new section. If that's not the effect that enabling "smooth scroll" does in ScrollSpy bootstrap 5.2 then I suggest the term be changed.

body:
The ScrollSpy rewrite just landed on the `v6-dev` branch (#42557). It's now driven by an `IntersectionObserver` activation line (no scroll-position polling), with deterministic active-section detection, correct URL-hash/focus handling on smooth scroll, and `getElementById`-based id resolution (so dotted/encoded ids work) — which addresses this.

Please give the new ScrollSpy a try on `v6-dev` and share any feedback here. There's still time to refine it, both now and through the alpha, so if the behavior isn't right for your case we'd like to hear about it.

Closing as resolved by the rewrite — reopen or comment if it still reproduces on `v6-dev`.

body:
> What version of Bootstrap are you using?
>
> v5.1.3

> The documentation shows usage for sticky-bottom, but the classes are not generated in bootstrap/scss/helpers/_position.scss file

[In 5.1.3, only `.sticky-top` is available](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.1/helpers/position/) - see [corresponding bootstrap.css](https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/bootstrap@5.1.3/dist/css/bootstrap.css) that only contains `.sticky-*-top` classes.
We can see that in the code shared in the issue where the rule is `.sticky#{$infix}-top`.

`.sticky-bottom` was added in 5.2.0 (see https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.2/helpers/position/#sticky-bottom) - see [corresponding bootstrap.css](https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/bootstrap@5.2.0-beta1/dist/css/bootstrap.css) that contains both `.sticky-*-top` and `.sticky-*-bottom` classes.

author: mdo

body:
The issue is not fixed, please reopen the ticket.

The `dispose()` method fails to reset scrollbars, leaving `overflow: hidden` on the `body` element intact. Neither `Offcanvas.js:dispose()` nor `Modal.js:dispose()` invoke `ScrollBarHelper().reset()`.

Currently, this reset is only triggered in the `hide()` logic of Bootstrap's Offcanvas/Modal. However, this approach is ineffective for us because Blazor (SPA) removes elements from the DOM, causing the `hide()` call to fail before `scrollbar.reset()` can be executed.
author: hakenr

body:
Steps to Reproduce:
1. Open (show) an Offcanvas/Modal within the SPA.
2. Navigate to a different screen within the SPA, which results in the removal of the Offcanvas/Modal elements from the DOM (for example, by clicking the BACK button in the browser).
3. During navigation, execute `dispose()` for cleanup. Since the elements are already being removed from the DOM, it's not possible to call `hide()` in this context.
4. Observe that `overflow:hidden` remains applied to the `body` element because `ScrollBarHelper.reset()` is not included in the disposal logic.

...the `dispose()` should do the cleanup even if the offcanvas/modal was shown.

body:
Scrollspy has clearly regressed, even on the docs. The prior behavior was to make a link active if its associated element reached the scroll top. Now, it seems to move the active state if one element is more visible than the other. This does not get the same results, and has very strange behavior in various different scenarios which expected the previous behavior. This is just the coded behavior, it's not browser dependent.

Here is an example of the difference on the docs page itself.

https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2672245/170849218-7faab47f-67e6-4295-8265-8544725b4d35.mp4
author: mastercoms

body:
Here's one example of how it broke. This is just me making the paragraph longer on the 5.2 docs.

https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2672245/170849451-0552fe36-70dd-4311-96e9-8de918a9631f.mp4

There's just no active link, since the heading is not visible. There is absolutely no way to handle this correctly with the current implementation, since if you have a wrapper element around each heading and paragraph, then it does not activate properly at all (since the large wrapper element will not satisfy the thresholds properly). And you can fiddle around with the root margin and maybe thresholds to help fit the content within the intersection parameters, but it will be specific to each section, so there is no setup that works with varying bits of spied content sections.

body:
I have been getting better results when wrapping the entire section that I want to spy on with the `id` target. For example, instead of doing this (example given in docs): ```

First heading

...

``` I will do this: ```

First heading

...

``` This way the `id` target is in the viewport for most of the time. This does seem to mess things up a bit when I have one section that is smaller than another that is also within the viewport. My implementation is very similar to the old Bootstrap 3 docs with nested nav items that appear when the parent has the active state (like the sidebar here https://getbootstrap.com/docs/3.4/components/) making it very important to have the parent being spied on at all time. I played around with the `rootMargin` property as well, similar to what was done in the docs, bringing it to `rootMargin: '0px 0px -35%'` in my case which seemed to help. Everything seems to be working when scrolling down, but when reaching the bottom of the page and scrolling back up, there are a lot of issues. The active item spied, when clicking on a menu link, after getting to the bottom of the page is inaccurate and sometimes no active item is picked up. Some items don't receive the active state at all, especially when scrolling back up the page versus clicking on a menu link. I am thinking that this has to do with the last scrollspy target section being very short in height, so it messes with the positioning when going back up. Just some observations and suggestions in case they help progress this issue further!

body: In my use case, ScrollSpy does not work with sections with large height by default, and l noticed that the default threshold `[0.1, 0.5, 1]`, typically `0.1`, is not good for large height section . For me, `data-bs-threshold="0,1" data-bs-root-margin="-30% 0% -70%"` fixed the issue. (EDIT: Adding `display: flow-root` to the sections is also important to disable gaps between sections caused by child margins.)

body:
Rolling back to an old, performance-intensive code with scroll events is **not** the way to go! It's clear that browsers [natively](https://caniuse.com/intersectionobserver) support this use-case with [Intersection Observer API](https://medium.com/@mz.ebrahimi/discover-the-magic-of-intersection-observer-bringing-web-interactivity-to-life-716c742d02ac) and we should not go back and reinvent the wheel.

What I'm trying to understand is if this is a bug, change on default behaviour or a skill issue. I was out of bootstrap community for many years, but I must say, a lot of developers around here are just complaining and demanding free stuff. What if you would try to contribute back? BS is open-source. Fork it, add tests, fix it and push it back for everybody to benefit!
author: razvanphp

body:
> Rolling back to an old, performance-intensive code with scroll events is **not** the way to go!

It absolutely is the way to go if the old code worked and the new code is broken—having it work correctly is more important than it being performant.

> What I'm trying to understand is if this is a bug, change on default behaviour or a skill issue.

It’s a bug. I’m not sure how it adds to the conversation to suggest it’s a skill issue.

> I was out of bootstrap community for many years, but I must say, a lot of developers around here are just complaining and demanding free stuff. What if you would try to contribute back? BS is open-source. Fork it, add tests, fix it and push it back for everybody to benefit!

Everyone here is reporting and discussing a bug, not complaining and demanding free stuff. Telling people not to report bugs unless they fix it themselves is not productive. And there are, in fact, multiple people working on PRs to fix it.

author: DenisLopatin

body:
The ScrollSpy rewrite just landed on the `v6-dev` branch (#42557). It's now driven by an `IntersectionObserver` activation line (no scroll-position polling), with deterministic active-section detection, correct URL-hash/focus handling on smooth scroll, and `getElementById`-based id resolution (so dotted/encoded ids work) — which addresses this.

Please give the new ScrollSpy a try on `v6-dev` and share any feedback here. There's still time to refine it, both now and through the alpha, so if the behavior isn't right for your case we'd like to hear about it.

Closing as resolved by the rewrite — reopen or comment if it still reproduces on `v6-dev`.
author: mdo

body:
## What is your goal? Keep the old title or just overwrite it?
dublicate to: https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/issues/32944

I couldn't find any line in the documentation talking about the `data-bs-original-title` attribute.
The problem you mentioned occured in bootstrap 4 according to the stackoverflow link you linked.
The title attribut is now responsible for displaying the tooltip content. If you first set the title and then initialize the tooltip, everything is working.

```js
document
  .getElementById("tt")
  .setAttribute("data-bs-title", "New Tooltip Title");
new bootstrap.Tooltip(jQuery('[data-toggle="tooltip"]'));
```
This was a solution mentioned in your stackoverflow link: https://stackoverflow.com/a/65863062/10192487
**However it should be possible to use `data-bs-title` because the options are saying that you can use the prefix `data-bs-` to adjust the options which working since 5.1 or 5.2.** [[doc ref](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.0/components/tooltips/#options)]

## Your examples seem to be a bit odd.

Your 5.0 example includes beta source Links which might not represent a stable behaviour.
Since 5.0 Tooltips are getting initialized like this `new bootstrap.Tooltip(jQuery('[data-toggle="tooltip"]'));` [[doc ref](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.0/components/tooltips/#example-enable-tooltips-everywhere)]

Bootstrap should according to documentation only initialize with jQuery by searching for the `jQuery` Object on window instead of `$`. [[doc ref](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.0/getting-started/javascript/#still-want-to-use-jquery-its-possible)]

Both of your button markups include the `data-placement` attribute which should be `data-bs-placement` in 5.0. [[doc ref](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.0/components/tooltips/#examples)]

body:
You can read more about why we removed this by starting at https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/issues/28506.

> In older versions of Bootstrap you could define just your additional color which got merged into the theme-colors list.
> Now days you have to copy all of the mappings (including level up variables) to add just one theme-color for example.. That's kinda annoying.

This isn't true. If you wanted to add another color to `$theme-colors`, you declare it after `_variables.scss` and before `_maps.scss` (in v5.2.0-beta1).

```scss
$theme-colors: map-merge(
  $theme-colors,
  (
    custom: $pink-500
  )
);
```

And if you want to remove something from the `$theme-colors`, you'd do this:

```scss
$theme-colors: map-remove($theme-colors, "primary");
```

author: mdo

body:
Hello @BoPeng!
Thanks for opening an issue :)

> In my case the values are returned from the backend as part of the invalid user input waiting to be revised, so I consider this a valid use case.

Sorry but I'm not sure to understand the use case precisely. Are you saying that for example you have an empty form, and then that this form is (pre) filled out automatically by some backend data?
If it is the case, in your CodePen, you should let `.needs-validation` and remove `.was-validated` since the form hasn't been submitted yet.

If your use case is more about server side validation, you should rather use [this example](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.2/forms/validation/#server-side).
author: julien-deramond

body:
In my case, the form was initially `.needs-validation`, user fills out some data and`POST` to the server. The server processes the data, returns the submitted form with `.need-validation` replaced by `.was-validated` and additional error messages.

So the returned form has 
1. `.was-validated` on `form`
2. Non-empty `input` with user-input data
3. `.invalid-feedback` from the server.

Number 2 is the only addition to the example. Since the documentation does not mention the role of `value` in `input`, it was quite a surprise to me that the `input` would be marked green, and the situation is weirder if the server marks the field `.is-invalid` (green input + red error message).

The solution, as you pointed out, is to avoid using `.was-validated` and stick to field-level `.is-valid` and `.is-invalid`. This is quite troublesome to me since there can be a large number of fields and `is-valid` and `is-invalid` can be clearly derived from the existence of `.invalid-feedback`, as the case for `.was-validated`.

author: jgriffin1

body:
We also encountered this problem today. We try to use the browser's own validations as much as possible. However, there are a few cases in which we cannot avoid to use a custom validation. Therefore we need the `was-validated` class in the form element to support the browser's own validations and the `is-invalid` class on the fields with custom validation.

We're using react with `react-hook-form`, which makes it difficult to use [setCustomValidity](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/HTMLObjectElement/setCustomValidity). 

A css-only solution to support the `:is-invalid` pseudo-class and the `is-invalid' class in the same form would be awesome.

body: v6-dev rewrote form validation around `:user-invalid`/`:user-valid` (behind `[data-bs-validate]`) instead of `.was-validated` + `:valid`/`:invalid`, so a pre-filled value no longer auto-validates or overrides `.is-invalid`. Closing as resolved in v6-dev — please open a new issue if it persists there.

author: GeoSot

body:
Hi there, 

We've been a bit struggling with that question since we weren't fully aware of what it would change and we didn't work a lot on this topic yet.

We both agree that it could be great to see this API for stable versions since it could help people build Bootstrap based components. But this means that there's a lot of work added by the API:
* No regressions on API versions
* Inform about each change (that could be a nightmare)
* Maybe add some documentation about the new features

On the topic of what need to be exported, based on Boosted custom components, we assume that every Js component needs at least `BaseComponent`, `EventHandler` and `SelectorEngine`. I personally haven't used `Manipulator` so I can't relate much about that. Moreover, if GeoSot thinks that we should keep some files private to Bootstrap because they aren't ready, I think that they should be kept private until ready.

This has nothing to do with floating labels, and is intended: [email inputs remain in LTR, as recommended in RTLstyling 101](https://rtlstyling.com/posts/rtl-styling#form-inputs).

If you want / need to override this, the code sits in [`_reboot.scss`](https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/blob/main/scss/_reboot.scss#L538-L550) and uses a RTLCSS directive.

author: Remzi1993

body:
From what I can see, Bootstrap doesn't expose any of Popper's API internals in a way that would prevent upgrading, which means it should be possible to keep it backwards-compatible. You can map the options to the new Floating UI ones with the same behavior after changing the dependency, and end-users wouldn't see any difference.

Cases where it'd break is non-bundled CDN usage where they reference `bootstrap@5` (non-pinned) which automatically loads in new versions, and it then depends on Floating UI link instead; or they didn't have `@popperjs/core` installed but were using it indirectly anyway. I'm not sure if either of those should be covered by semver. 

Upgrading should take less than an hour overall. Disclaimer: I am a maintainer of Floating UI. If you need help let me know!

Edit: Just realized I was on `4.0` docs accidentally, not `5.0`. In v5, I can see there's a `popperConfig` object which complicates it quite a bit, although it's still possible to map the behavior but needs a middle layer to keep the API the same.

  --bs-btn-padding-y: 0.375rem;
  --bs-btn-font-family: ;
                      ^
Unrecognised input
      Error in node_modules\bootstrap\dist\css\bootstrap.css (line 2809, column 24)
```

In version 5.1.3 there were no empty variables. Now, in version 5.2.0 there are. That change breaks things and so we will have to downgrade back to version 5.1.3.

Even if empty css variables are "technically ok"- many interpreters don't think so.
Instead of contacting all of those interpreters and having them change their software- it may be better if bootstrap just doesn't make use of empty css variables.

body:
Thanks for opening an issue @jhthompson :)

> I am willing to open a PR to fix, but could not find source file to edit (is there a v5.0 branch to make the PR against?)

You can create a branch against our _main_ branch and modify [migration.md](https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/blob/main/site/content/docs/5.2/migration.md).
If the modification is approved/merged, the latest version of the 5.x documentation will contain your modification in "Migrating to v5".
However I'm not sure if we will be able to modify as well the already shipped migration guide for 5.1 and 5.0.

body:
Thanks for opening this issue @mjablecnik.

Indeed `rounded-top` defines `border-top-right-radius: var(--bs-border-radius) !important; border-top-left-radius: var(--bs-border-radius) !important; }` while `rounded-5` defines the rounded for all borders `border-radius: var(--bs-border-radius-2xl) !important;`.

We could maybe create `.rounded-{top|end|bottom|start}-{0-5}` (in your example using `.rounded-top-5` instead of `.rounded-top .rounded-5`) by doing in our `scss/_utilities.scss`:
```scss
"rounded-top": (
    property: border-top-left-radius border-top-right-radius,
    class: rounded-top,
    values: (
    null: var(--#{$prefix}border-radius),
    0: 0,
    1: var(--#{$prefix}border-radius-sm),
    2: var(--#{$prefix}border-radius),
    3: var(--#{$prefix}border-radius-lg),
    4: var(--#{$prefix}border-radius-xl),
    5: var(--#{$prefix}border-radius-2xl),
    circle: 50%,
    pill: var(--#{$prefix}border-radius-pill)
  )
),
```
What do you think @mdo about adding it? Gave it a try in the draft PR https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/pull/36540.

body:
This isn't exactly broken, it's that the Sass variables are `null` and do not compile to anything. This works great in our normal property usage (Sass will remove the `property: null` line during compilation), but not so well with CSS variables. We may need to add a manual check for these things in the future.

#35442 aimed to try that out, but I wasn't too keen on the extra syntax on top of each instance.
author: mdo

body:
FWIW, this is valid CSS. 🤷

The only potential issue is the predictability regarding the behaviour, since every property will inherit and some won't (but again, this is the standard behaviour when you don't provide any value).

Dropping them would decrease file size a little bit, but I'm not sure it'd be noticeable.

@patrickhlauke it caused beacuse of
```html
<div class="offcanvas-lg offcanvas-end flex-grow-1 show" id="bdNavbar" aria-labelledby="bdNavbarOffcanvasLabel" data-bs-scroll="true" aria-modal="true" role="dialog">
```
According to code:
https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/blob/2bf383c59640a0c693a4ab6b5dc58104c5ec2294/js/src/offcanvas.js#L115-L122
Focustrap is activated only when option `scroll= false`

So possible solutions:
  * just change the option
  * re-define the option effects

author: GeoSot

body:
Hello

With that modification focustrap is activated when scroll is disabled **or** when it has backdrop (the last condition is the new one)

Previous code was (v5.1.3):
https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/blob/1a6fdfae6be09b09eaced8f0e442ca6f7680a61e/js/src/offcanvas.js#L120-L123

New code (from v5.2.0):
https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/blob/edf9c40956d19e6ab3f9151bfe0dfac6be06fa21/js/src/offcanvas.js#L116-L119

This change breaks this use case (working in previous versions):

1. An offcanvas is used to show a form on it 
    - 'scroll=true' to assure 'focustrap.activate()' is not called
    -  backdrop=true, to assure other elements outside the offcanvas are selected by user
2. Some action in that form opens a pop-up (to gather more info/data)
    - Form elements in that pop-up were accesible to user (get focus, write...)

With current modification, input elements in that pop-up NEVER can get focus, becasue offcanvas trapped interaction when called: `this._focustrap.activate()`

If previous behavior can't be recovered... Could be possible to publish a property ("focustrap") to allow developer to choose if oncanvass performs that logic? ('this._focustrap.activate()/deactivate()' )
Other options are welcome

Regards
author: floss-dev

body:
> Some action in that form opens a pop-up (to gather more info/data)

just on that point, i'll note that offcanvas is an offshoot of a modal, and bootstrap doesn't support modals called from within modals by default
author: patrickhlauke

body:
Yes @patrickhlauke, that seems the be the new behaviour from v5.2.0
Meanwhile, previous developments/usages are impacted by the change (almost, when upgrading Bootstrap)

I think some level of backwards compatibility could be allowed (and will be welcome):
  + new property, as proposed in previous comment
  + publish functions to manipulate focustrap (activate/deactivate)
  + others…

Regards

body:
From memory, the old behavior is an old remnant/side effect from an early version of Bootstrap (possibly before version 4?) that was never properly documented.

The only official documentation regarding cursor keys appeared back in version 4  https://getbootstrap.com/docs/4.6/components/dropdowns/#accessibility

> However, Bootstrap does add built-in support for most standard keyboard menu interactions, such as the ability to move through individual `.dropdown-item` elements using the cursor keys and close the menu with the `ESC` key.

And I seem to remember the original code was quite hacky in terms of having to override normal keyboard behavior inside form fields that happen to be in a dropdown (e.g. things like textareas, where all of a sudden cursor keys didn't work as they should because of the override).

While I understand that having this undocumented feature helped create some interesting/creative approaches, like the ajax search above, note that those approaches are not fully "correct" from an accessibility point of view - you'd really want to implement them along these lines https://www.w3.org/WAI/ARIA/apg/patterns/combobox/

Even just for dropdowns with links, the current additional cursor navigation is "non-standard" really, but left there as a nice-to-have extra that doesn't interfere with regular accessibility of those dropdowns (which, in essence, are simply expand/collapse panels in the ARIA sense).

In short, I would suggest not reintroducing the old behavior and noting it as a change (but not a breaking change, since this was never an explicit/official feature of BS it seems).

author: mlandalv

body:
I got troubles understanding the issue precisely with my first tests and had a chat with @Lausselloic (thanks for that) to have more details.

If my understanding is right we have 3 use cases:
1. [The first one](https://codepen.io/julien-deramond/pen/poZmeyZ) loads `bootstrap.min.css` file in the main document. So the web component has access to the CSS vars defined in `:root` but doesn't have access to the `.accordion*` classes. So in terms of rendering, accordions are broken but the div with colors is OK.
2. [The second one](https://codepen.io/julien-deramond/pen/gOjJmwW) loads `bootstrap.min.css` file in the main document AND in the web component. So the web component has access to the CSS vars defined in `:root` because the CSS file is loaded in the main document, and the `.accordion*` classes work because of the loading of Bootstrap CSS file within the web component.
3. Finally [the third one](https://codepen.io/julien-deramond/pen/MWBdpbz) only loads `bootstrap.min.css` within the web component; and it seems to be what's this issue is about right? For example, a use case would be that the main document loading Material or whatever, and the web component would use Bootstrap component and style. In this case, indeed, CSS vars defined in `:root` are not accessible (the div with colors remains black on white bg) and the accordion style doesn't work fully because it also depends on some CSS vars defined in `:root`. This is why we need `:root, :host` in this case so that these CSS vars are accessible.

Don't hesitate to tell me if this is not correct. While waiting for feedback, I'll continue to work on https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/pull/37162 with this latest example in mind.

However, as pointed by @mlandalv, it won't fix everything. It's going to be better but not enough. Not sure what would be the proper fix for that.

body: My two cents: if you're using Bootstrap for your components styles, markup could definitely sit in the Light DOM. That's how [Lion](https://lion-web.netlify.app) delivers white label Web Components, thus allows any styling strategy.
author: ffoodd

body:
Having custom properties defined on `:host` will make boostrap work inside a web component but you won't be able to customize the component through those same properties. Those declared in the component could not be ovewritten from "outside".
It could be intended, or not, depending on the kind of component youre are doing. If you don't want your web component to be customizable, it's a good thing. But if you make a webcomponent in order to replace Bootstrap JS, maybe you want to inherit website custom properties values.

**EDIT** : I played with custom properties and you obviously can override component custom properties from the "outside", they need to be set on the custom element itself (that would be a problem for customizing component if we could not)

author: lcsqlpete

body:
modal dialogs need to contain their focus. once a modal is open, focus is not allowed to go out of it (e.g. into the underlying page behind the semi-transparent overlay) to conform with https://www.w3.org/WAI/ARIA/apg/patterns/dialogmodal/.

newer versions of bootstrap strictly enforce this. that can cause issues with other widgets that dynamically generate things like popovers etc if these live outside of the modal dialog.

try providing a container inside the modal, and explicitly pointing the popover to use that https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.2/components/popovers/#custom-container - and in light of https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.2/components/popovers/#making-popovers-work-for-keyboard-and-assistive-technology-users make sure that container follow immediately after the "Add" button.

as an aside, note that your "Add" button can't be activated using the keyboard, it seems.

and the `tabindex="-1"` on the modal is needed to make sure that it receives focus once it's opened, and for the correct focus trapping.

I was stuck in same problem while upgrading my old theme along with plugins.
The solution is
when you initialize the popover that is inside a popover in a modal.
you have to specify the container of the popover 


` $('#my-popover').popover{
container: 'modal' // <== Just add this to make it focusable.
}`

body:
I would really like to have these by default, without having to add them myself. I typically use `.container-fluid.mw-md` instead of `.container` for two reasons:

- The biggest break point is too wide for many use cases.
- I do not want to waste space between break points.

See also #22008 for some arguments. I don't think `.container` should be removed. But these `.mw-*` classes are an obvious solution and should absolutely be available by default.

body:
Having difficulty replicating this in CodePen even though it's 100% reproducible in my SvelteKit app. This is closer to the full example... https://codepen.io/nstuyvesant/pen/poLRNpb?editors=1010.

Several things have to happen for the problem to occur:
1. You must use Bootstrap 5.2.0-beta1.
2. The modal should be opened via JavaScript rather than data attributes.
3. The modal must have at least one input.
4. Buttons must be in the modal-footer. The cancel button can use data attributes to dismiss the modal.
5. Modal content must be so long that the buttons in the modal-footer are not initially visible.
6. The methods .focus() or .select() are called on an input on 'shown.bs.modal' event.

With this combination of things, clicking buttons in the modal-footer will scroll the modal and set the focus on the close button.

I even tried
```
    setTimeout(() => {
      const title = document.getElementById('title')
      title?.focus()
    }, 5000)
```

If I scroll to the modal-footer before the timeout of 5 seconds, the buttons work normally. Once focus() is called, the odd behavior begins.

body:
The button variants are handled with a mixin based on the contrast of our background and foreground colors. We darken or lighten based on if the contrast would be lessened on hover/active/etc. It's not always a guarantee that the hover and active states will consistently darken or lighten, which ultimately gives the system some more flexibility.

That said, we did ship some tweaks to buttons in v5.2.0 stable today, so hopefully that helps you out a bit with the dark and light buttons in particular.
author: mdo

body:
> When I click the <a> tag, the offcanvas closes, but the tag doesn't lead me to /foo.

This is not a bug :/ . By design `data-bs-dismiss` has to do one job => close the offcanvas and do not affect something else

So if you want to achieve both behaviors, I think you workaround is the 'right' one
author: GeoSot

body:
> This is not a bug :/ . By design `data-bs-dismiss` has to do one job => close the offcanvas and do not affect something else

@GeoSot Umm...so `href` of an anchor tag by design does nothing if it has `data-bs-dismiss`? If it is made to *not affect something else*, I think it has to have no effect on `href` as well i.e. `href` should work. May I have some more explanations?
author: TrulyBright

body:
`href` or `data-bs-target` are used to target a specific element https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.2/components/offcanvas/#toggle

And if you check this line, which is responsible for handling the dismiss action you will notice a check to prevent default events 
https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/blob/ed448925daa4fb20344f94e35c37dded9e0af8af/js/src/util/component-functions.js#L14-L18
author: GeoSot

body: Oh, so `href` works as an *indicator*, not a hyperlink, if the tag is used as a bootstrap component. I see. Sorry to bother you and thank you for such careful explanation :)

body:
Check the Doc at: [https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.2/customize/sass/#importing](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.2/customize/sass/#importing)
Download: [https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.2/getting-started/download/#source-files](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.2/getting-started/download/#source-files)
From the file: **bootstrap-5.2.2/scss/bootstrap.scss**
**Copy from** _// scss-docs-start import-stack_ 
**Copy to** _// scss-docs-end import-stack_ 
to your new scss main file and set all the @import ""; to **correct path.**
**Ex:** 
**@import "functions";** -> **@import "../../../../../themes/contrib/bootstrap5/dist/bootstrap/5.2.2/scss/functions";**

**Note:-** Comment all unnecessary functionality that you are not using. So that your compiled file will be as small as possible.

body:
I'm going to close this and leave it as-is for now. The difference is quite subtle right now, and the purpose of these global styles is to _limit_ further per-component customization. Lightening the `border-color` would address a small color layering issue, but introduces new complexity to the overall system. It also reduces the contrast in the cards with `.bg-*`/`.bg-text-*` utilities applied.

I'll keep this in mind though for future theming updates—definitely want a more nuanced color system like the one I'm developing in #35857.

author: julien-deramond

body:
Some extra-information about this issue. With Autoprefixer 10.4.7, there are some cases where `color-adjust: exact;` is added.

It can be tested directly in https://autoprefixer.github.io/.

As an example:
```css
.example {
    print-color-adjust: exact;
}
```

* With the basic rule proposed by the website for `browserlist`, `color-adjust: exact` is not added.
* With the content of our [`.browserlistrc`](https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/blob/main/.browserslistrc) (`last 2 major versions, not dead, Chrome >= 60, Firefox >= 60, Firefox ESR, iOS >= 12, Safari >= 12, not Explorer <= 11`), `color-adjust: exact` is added.
* If we remove the rules regarding Firefox from our [`.browserlistrc`](https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/blob/main/.browserslistrc) (`last 2 major versions, not dead, Chrome >= 60, iOS >= 12, Safari >= 12, not Explorer <= 11`), `color-adjust: exact` is not added.

If we change `autoprefixer` version in our `package.json` without changing our [`.browserlistrc`](https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/blob/main/.browserslistrc):
* 10.4.5: nothing is added
* 10.4.6: `-webkit-print-color-adjust: exact;` and `-moz-print-color-adjust: exact;` are added
* 10.4.7: `-webkit-print-color-adjust: exact;` and `color-adjust: exact;` are added

We can see a warning message in our proper build when we run `css-rtl`. It makes run a second time PostCSS + Autoprefixer.
That must be the same issue if a user run PostCSS + Autoprefixer on something based on the CSS file we deliver and that contains `color-adjust: exact;`.

I still doesn't understand why in one hand `color-adjust: exact` is added to support Firefox < 97 and on the other hand, a file already containing a `color-adjust: exact` to support Firefox < 97 generates a warning.
author: julien-deramond

body: @julien-deramond Some great investigative work there. Some very confusing contributing factors here that make it hard to know what the final fix is. But, good to see the cause of the property has been discovered.

author: mdo

body:
Hi @maureenblack -- I hear you say that you want to an element's style based on the screen width (aka, current break point). If BS were to support the change of text color based on break point, there would then be the need to support background-color and font weight, etc. This would be hard to support and make the BS markup on elements messy. There already exists easy to use methods to accomplish what you want. METHOD 1 is good if you want minor changes (like Text color). METHOD 2 is good if you have substantial changes. METHOD 1: Not related to BS, is the use of media queries. This works very well and lets you change any style based on the screen width and gives you everything (and more!) of what you are looking for. https://codepen.io/rjpaulsen/pen/xxWdMMq You could easily expand the example to reflect Bootstrap's break points (listed on this page): https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.2/layout/breakpoints/ METHOD 2: If you have drastic changes between screen resolutions, the BS "Display" property could be used to toggle different screen elements based on the BS breakpoints. For example you could use an `
Elements formatted for large screens
Elements formatted for small screens
``` Several examples here: https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.2/utilities/display/

body: > > Apparently, it comes from [328f723](https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/commit/328f723008cc39292a9f355e2eafb0fd04740656). > > You are right on this. It was something we had discussed together too, as the title was just left empty. > > @BrokenSourceCode I would prefer to not 'fix' this as it wasn't documented and of course is a bad practice to leave a blank attribute there. So you could, may use any other attr No problem, I'll use another selector if you want to keep the code as it is. Perhaps it should be mentioned inside the `selector` option [documentation](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.2/components/tooltips/#options) that the `title` attribute cannot be used as a selector, right? Since this has changed since Bootstrap 5.2. This could be useful in the future for developers who encounter the same issue as me. Maybe like this: ```diff If a selector is provided, tooltip objects will be delegated to the specified targets. In practice, this is used to also apply tooltips to dynamically added DOM elements (jQuery.on support). See this issue and an informative example. + Note that this option cannot work with the title attribute (such as [title]), because the attribute is removed from the DOM by Bootstrap. ```
author: ghost

body: @GeoScot It is documented in https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.2/components/tooltips/#options and really does need to be fixed as its a regression from several previous releases. Dynamically generated content is pretty common now, and `title="something"` is a useful fallback is something goes wrong
