body:
@mdo

> Shipping a whole new behavior to anyone who updates in a minor release feels like an overreach.

I agree. Maybe this entire "color modes" feature should wait until version 6?

> The vast majority of websites built on Bootstrap likely don't have dark mode, and surprising them with it doesn't feel right.

This statement seems to conflate the lack of a feature with the lack of desire or preparedness.

> Our JS color mode picker that we use in our own docs defaults to automatically changing the color mode to match the OS color mode. This gives developers and users flexibility. Developers can pick what modes to include, while users can use their OS defaults or select an override as their new default

`prefers-color-scheme` and `prefers-contrast` also offers flexibility to developers and users. Developers can choose which schemes and contrast their site supports via meta attributes or CSS. Users can choose via their browser (or OS) defaults. Bootstrap's "color modes" seem to reinvent the wheel in this regard.

My company wants to offer dark and light color schemes on our websites using Bootstrap, but the current implementation only seems to give us two options:

* Write a custom "color mode" picker from scratch in JavaScript and maintain it for the foreseeable future
* Recompile Bootstrap's Sass from scratch, and then recompile it again every time an update comes out.

Most of my company's websites are written in C# using ASP.NET Core, like dan-giddins. So we don't have much experience with JavaScript or Sass

author: mdo

body:
I found the following problems that don't respect the current theme:

1. Emphasis, subtle and border-subtle are derived from the theme colors themselves. Defining them as SCSS variables only causes problems downstream. Should be calculated and set in CSS vars only.
2. "Light" and "Dark" don't really make sense in light, dark or whatever themes at all, especially seeing how they can be considered their own themes. I suggest these to be removed altogether from the theme colors.
3. "Primary" is used pretty consistently as an accent and an active color. Secondary, on the other hand, is used almost interchangeably with shades of gray. "Secondary", thus, shouldn't be available to configure either.
4. Wherever `$primary` is referenced for example in CSS rules, it won't respect the current theme. Some very serious culprits here are `$component-active-bg: $primary !default;` and `$focus-ring-color: rgba($primary, $focus-ring-opacity) !default;`. These should refer to `var(--#{prefix}primary)` instead, but it'll cause some additional problems, such as `$input-focus-border-color: tint-color($component-active-bg, 50%) !default;` - this expects a value at build time to function correctly.
author: yugabe

body: Different, but related I think: setting something like `$headings-color` doesn't work as expected. Imagine setting `$body-color` to, say, `$gray-700`, and `$headings-color` to `$gray-900`. You end up with the wrong heading color in, for example, active list group items: Card titles seems to escape the problem, but only because a) `.card-title` has higher specificity than `h1`, etc., and b) the `.card-title` color is "wrong" — i.e., the same as `$body-color` rather than darker: Setting `$card-title-color` to `$headings-color` fixes that, but breaks other things: If I'm doing things the wrong way, obviously I don't know what the right way is. :-) This isn't a theming issue per se; all of these examples fall into the "light" theme. But. But it seems like some concept is missing. I can't define one universal headings color when that color will be used on various backgrounds? It seems like anywhere you can define a color — at least, a text color — you need to be able to define two: one for use on "light" backgrounds, and one for use on "dark" backgrounds. The contrast ratio helper would then choose which one to use. Or something like that.

body:
This is super interesting, thanks for sharing! We're definitely in a weird place with v5.3.0 with theming—it's both powerful and slightly cumbersome. We've known it wasn't the complete solution for awhile and that's what we aim to address most in v6. We've talked about reimagining theme settings with a new map as well:

```scss
$theme: (
  "colors": (
    "primary": $primary,
    "secondary": $secondary,
    "success": $success,
    "info": $info,
    "warning": $warning,
    "danger": $danger,
  ),
  
  "fgs": (
    "default": $body-color,
    "secondary": $body-secondary,
    "tertiary": $body-tertiary,
  ),

  "bgs": (
    "default": $body-bg,
    "secondary": $body-bg-secondary,
    "tertiary": $body-bg-tertiary,
  ),

  "borders": (
    "default": $body-border,
    "secondary": $body-border-secondary,
    "tertiary": $body-border-tertiary,
  ),
) !default;
```

But adding in another layer for each theme, and iterating over those themes, is also really cool. Finding a middle ground here would be tremendous in v6.
author: mdo

body:
Thanks for considering this, @mdo! 

I also considered the secondary-tertiary colors, but I think they can be inferred from the defaults. Overriding all of those could be done as well, but maybe only via CSS variables, as the reuse of those variables across user code would provide issues that make Bootstrap non-responsive of theme changes currently.

Having a "minimal configuration, good defaults" policy could go a long way, if a way to override those defaults is also provided.

In my final example above, I intentionally avoided the `$primary`, `$secondary`, `$success`, `$info`, `$warning`, and `$danger` variables. These variables are a bit misguided in my opinion, because they refer to discreet colors in a static palette, so they cannot be reused anywhere in the code except where the theme is being defined. But at that point, it's easier to just skip them and refer to the colors themselves. Secondly, and seeing as how the default palette's colors (still, `$primary` and co.) are essentially only a map of string-colors, the user can be left to define the keys/names for these colors.


Consider the following part of the example:

```scss
  "blue": (
    "body-bg": $blue-100,
    "body-color": $gray-900,
    "accent-1": $blue-600,
    "accent-2": $cyan-300,
    "error": $purple-200
  )
```

This means that components that support theming would gain (at least) `accent-1`, `accent-2`, `error`, variants, like `alert-accent-2`. Moving the "default" variables, like `body-bg` to another named map, like you said, would be a good way to define these.

I have to say that the only things that shouldn't be allowed as variables are discreet colors. Considering all colors are part of a theme, it might be a good practice to never allow SCSS variables to have a discreet color value (except maybe the variables that refer to the colors themselves, like `$blue`). Other variables, like spacings or transforms are not affected, because they can be considered constant regardless of theme. 

---

As for me, unfortunately, I have to move away from using Bootstrap in my current project, because I think it's going to be extremely hard to change the current behaviors, even in v6. Some places use the `$primary` variable directly or indirectly (`$component-active-bg` is set to `$primary` as well). Whatever uses these variables or depend on them, cannot be themed. What's even more problematic, is that there are places where these values are needed at compile time, so they cannot be refactored easily either:

```scss
$form-range-thumb-active-bg:               tint-color($component-active-bg, 70%) !default;
```

The solution to this would be to refactor all colors INTO the theme declaration. Obviously, that would make the theme definition very large. Hence, the defaults should be inferred as much as possible and refer to a limited set of colors in the theme (or prefer the use of opacity where possible). 

I tried to fix these issues at usercode level, but it's simply not feasible. I started fixing it at the framework level (inside Bootstrap's SCSS), but it's too woven into the fabric that it is a huge undertaking that it's simply not viable for me to invest another two weeks of my time. Because of this, I have to cut my losses here and move on, but before that I wanted to open a few issues so that you guys at Bootstrap can consider moving in this direction if you feel it aligns with your goals.
author: yugabe
Karl1b

body: @Karl1b Yes, that's been the intention with our migration to CSS variables, but we're not 100% there due to technical issues and concerns around breaking changes.
author: mdo

body:
I personally think that $primary, $secondary, $success, $info, $warning, and $danger are essential to the way bootstrap has been built for a long time. Changing the names of these common variables to something like accent-1 and error does nothing to change the functionality so it's a breaking change in the way people have used bootstrap for no gain. There has to be a better reason to change naming than just that you like it better.

Also, removing secondary is a bad idea too. It's not just another gray. It's gray in the documentation site but not everyone uses it that way.

I really do like the idea of having a map of themes. It's a great idea. But I think we should decouple the renaming of variables from that idea as they are really unrelated. Adding the maps, even with no other changes would add value. That can stand on its own merit without anything else IMO.

@use "bootstrap" with (

    $secondary: #b4bcc2,
    $success: #18bc9c,
    $info: #3498db,
    $warning: #f39c12,
    $danger: #e74c3c
);
```

author: julien-deramond

body:
"To be absolutely clear, properties must have a value according to the rules. A property without a value is an error and will be ignored."

Problem is with web accesibility readers. I don't care about your bootstrap.css, problem is when I use this css on CDN servers.

In issue #36595 says: "This isn't exactly broken, it's that the Sass variables are null and do not compile to anything"

This is not right, Sass variables that are null are compiles not to anything, These null compiles to "--bs-btn-font-family: ;" for example. Sass is not removing the property: null line during compilation. It's still there and empty.

It happend only 16 times in https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/bootstrap@5.2.2/dist/css/bootstrap.min.css and 16 times in https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/bootstrap@5.2.3/dist/css/bootstrap.min.css

It's seems easy to fix it, doesn't it?
author: Ricardo-a11y

body:
To be crystal clear, there's nothing to fix as mentioned in the related issue. [This is valid CSS](https://www.w3.org/TR/css-variables/#guaranteed-invalid).

This is is not a property but a custom property, meant to be called somewhere at computed time. An empty value can even be used to [implement conditional CSS using a trick called Space Toggle](https://lea.verou.me/2020/10/the-var-space-hack-to-toggle-multiple-values-with-one-custom-property/). The Stack Overflow discussion you linked is 10 years old, custom properties didn't even exist at that time.

Empty custom property does not have any effect on screen readers or any other technology by itself. Whichever tool currently asserting you this is an issue is wrong and you should probably report an issue to them instead.

author: Xcreen

body:
Hi from 2025! I have the same issue, is there any progress?

Also, can someone please explain, why focusing the trigger back is mandatory for keyboard users?

(I want to understand this.)

### Scenario 1

- I have a single page
- the offcanvas holds a menu list with anchor links pointing to elements on that pages
- the user opens the offcanvas and focuses / clicks on an anchor link and the page scrolls down
- the user now closes the offcanvas, the trigger button is focused "back" and the page scrolls back up

### Scenario 2

- offcanvas is open, scrolling is enabled
- user scrolls down und sees something "interesting" and wants to read
- user closes the offcanvas (because he wants to read the "interesting" content), 
- now the trigger button is focused back and the page scrolls back up

**So why is this behavior considered mandatory for keyboard user?**
author: mockmeup

body:
in both scenarios, where is the keyboard user's focus after they close? if the press Space or hit Tab, where are they? without explicitly managing it, their focus is undefined/lost (which, among other things, fails WCAG 2.4.3 Focus Order)

if you have a more natural/appropriate place for focus to land after closing, explicitly define it and jump to it. otherwise, the default stands as the only sensible choice (of course, unless you just don't care about keyboard users/WCAG)
author: patrickhlauke

body:
I have a question for keyboard users: with `data-bs-scroll` enabled, how do you scroll the background? How do you even _get_ to the background with just Tab or Shift+Tab?

If you set `data-bs-scroll="true"`, aren't you inherently saying "Hey, I'm a mouse (or at least hybrid) user" …?

Without, or before there was offcanvas, the active element didn't have to change either after scrolling away with the mouse – it silently stayed on the now off-screen element. If you were to hit tab, you'd get to the next tabbable element. That's slightly confusing the first time it happens, but it makes sense enough that users get quickly accustomed.

Scrolling back down after you've scrolled away up (or vice versa) makes no sense at all.
author: WoodrowShigeru

body:
> If you set `data-bs-scroll="true"`, aren't you inherently saying "Hey, I'm a mouse (or at least hybrid) user" …?

correction: if you as the *author* set `data-bs-scroll="true"`, aren't you inherently saying "Hey, *I*'m assuming all my *users* are mouse (or at least hybrid) users"
author: patrickhlauke

body:
Fair, I will correct: if you as the author set `data-bs-scroll="true"`, aren't you saying "Hey, with this particular scroll feature of our offcanvas module, I support a particular convenience for our mouse users. This is for you, mouse users. Keyboard users _cannot_ use this feature." — Except, also this is true: "I immediately nullify that convenience with this annoying quirk".

Can't you silently restore that focus on the triggering element without scrolling into view? Where else is the focus supposed to be after closing? On `document.body`? You don't have to hold a neonsign pointing at it. I'm willing to bet, on their next Tab, users will only get confused the first time – but then quickly get accustomed to it because it makes sense enough to be there if you think about it.
author: WoodrowShigeru

body:
this is because of

```css
.d-flex {display: flex!important}
```

I've solved for myself using inline `style="display:flex"` instead of class `d-flex` for that case.

Or one can fix it with additional class `d-flex-ni` that not important.
```css
.d-flex-ni {display:flex}
```

Anyway If there is no solution or it will break something, you better mark in docs how to solve this. I wasted 40-60 minutes to get to the point (yeah, yeah I'm really noob in css).

body:
Disabling a feature only because of SPA feels bad, since it's clearly a minority of sites on the web—and the fact that any SPA framework out there imply a build chain, making handling Sass custom build for Bootstrap pretty easy.

There are however several workarounds to mitigate this: using a custom property to toggle this for example, and maybe for V6 scoping this style with something like `:has(:focus-visible)`.
author: ffoodd

body:
If smooth scrolling was objectively better, it would be the default browser behavior. Overriding that default is purely aesthetical choice and I'm not convinced Bootstrap should be opinionated about that.

Do people really like the animation after page load in [Bootstrap 5 docs](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/getting-started/introduction/#responsive-meta-tag)  more than the instantaneous scroll in [Bootstrap 4 docs](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/4.1/getting-started/introduction/#important-globals)?
author: zbynek

body:
Bootstrap is all about aesthetic choices (think colors or type scale), and a customisation layer thanks to Sass. 

The fact is v5 is around for about 3 years, downloaded millions times, and there're only a few comments and this issue regarding smooth scroll.

We had dozens of issues asking for it, or reporting that it wasn't working (meaning it was expected).

Moreover there's a really simple way to disable it through Sass (which IMO should be the standard way to use Bootstrap for performance reasons).

And I came with a few suggestions to ease disabling it, discussions are still open. I understand the issue, and any other opinions and ideas are welcome.

body:
I don't think absolutely positioning the footer like this is the modern way to achieve a "sticky" footer. You can use flexbox/grid to get the same result.
For example via Bootstrap's helper classes:
```html
<body class="min-vh-100 vstack">

  <header>
    this is the header
  </header>

  <main class="flex-grow-1">
    main content area
  </main>

  <footer>
    footer goes here
  </footer>

</body>
```

author: mdo

body:
This is unclear in the docs Indeed, but you're seeing the intended behaviour: `state` and other properties in the Utilities API are meant to extend utilities, without the need to add specific  entries for states, responsive variants, RTL conditions, etc.

To get hover state on `background-color`, you only need to extend existing `.bg-*` utitilies with the `state` property. `-hover` et all get appended to utility class name and doubled with equivalent pseudo-class, meaning you need to set `bg-primary-hover` to apply a primary colored background on hover.

Would this work for you?

I think the docs need some clarifications regarding properties and how to use them.
author: ffoodd

julien-deramond

body: The docs explain how to extend a utility to [enable responsable variants](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.2/utilities/api/#enable-responsive). Adding states is the same, using the `state` property.

body:
You might have had issues because you use both exclusive approaches to include Bootstrap Sass files. Based on our [Cutomize > Sass](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.2/customize/sass/#importing) docs, it's either `@import "~bootstrap/scss/bootstrap";` ("Include all of Bootstrap"), either including parts of Bootstrap.
I haven't tried in details your use case so maybe it's useful here but it sounded strange to me when I read your last comment.
But this is cool if you succeeded to make it work! Neat!

body: Sorry, my point is the buttons generate new colors with Sass for each button state. On hover, the background-color changes to be darker, and then even darker for active state. These extra colors (which apply to borders too) can't be regenerated with CSS, only with Sass right now. So for example we can shade the primary blue in Sass, but we can't in CSS. That means that even if the primary button background color is blue, we can't programmatically use CSS to create the darker blues for hover or active states.
author: mdo

body:
With the new color mode and the integration of custom properties, v5.3.0 will be somewhere in between. We have to break a lot of things IMO in order to provide better customization so it would be rather in v6.
We have ideas to improve this customization but unfortunately, it's impossible without breaking changes that can happen only in a major version.

Changing the main colors is important for simple customization for end-users, but we also have to maintain fine customization per component. Moreover, custom properties have some limitations that will be complicated to handle (see this recent discussion in https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/issues/38170#issue-1610313554 for example). But we will try things :)

body:
Thanks for this feature request @stijn7621.

I know that we talked about something like this during the implementation of the color mode with @mdo.
My personal opinion was that I don't see why it would be more helpful when you customize Bootstrap.
Copying and pasting `@include set-var()` instead of `--#{$prefix}` is as repetitive.
It would bring up something very specific to Bootstrap instead of using the regular declaration of custom properties. I'm not so sure in terms of readability and usage that it would be better TBH.
Moreover, in the documentation (e.g. https://twbs-bootstrap.netlify.app/docs/5.3/components/accordion/#variables), we would have a list of `@include set-var()` which doesn't look like custom properties.

But this is only my own opinion, let's see what others think about it :)

@Blueclaus13, before launching any dev, let's wait if we agree on this new feature

author: owaisahmed5300

body:
Hi @tyrex1975,
Thank you for your input. However, I would like to mention that the widget is built using jQuery, and in some projects, we do not load jQuery. Additionally, I noticed that the widget uses fixed selectors, which means that it cannot be reused and is fixed to specific elements and selectors. When developing a jQuery plugin, it is considered a best practice to make it reusable to more elements, and chainable by returning $this variable in the end.
author: owaisahmed5300

body: I assume you read my full message 'quick and dirty' and 'use at your own risk', but thanks for the unsolicited lesson in software dev all the same.  I thought I'd post as it might help some shortcut their work, but obviously not you, perhaps you'd like everything done for you so you don't have to code at all. 🙄 Anyway, I've remove it now, to save you from having to look at my crappy code.

body:
Hi @tyrex1975,
Thank you for that and I really appreciate that you are helping me and other members here. I added my note as most of the developers may just copy and paste snippets and suppose the code would work exactly as they required, It was just a reminder, that they need to modify the code to make it work as selectors are defined within the plugin, so if they try to call out the plugin on different selectors outside the plugin, it won't work and they need to change this inside the plugin.

Of course, The code you provided was good, We cannot say it a crap really, That was just a comment consisting of a piece of code to help the members how they can achieve that, My note was just for other people and anyone who can repost a non-jquery version of that to help people who are looking for jquery-free version.

Happy Coding :)

author: secuflag

body:
It seems that the issue you're experiencing may be related to the default font-size of the text in the paragraphs. When you add more paragraphs to the card body, the font-size of the text appears to increase slightly, causing the text to appear bigger than expected.

To resolve this, you can add a CSS rule to explicitly set the font-size of the text within the card body. Add the following code to your CSS file:

.card-body p {
font-size: 1rem; /* Or adjust to your desired size */
}

This should set the font-size of all paragraphs within the card body to a consistent size, regardless of how many paragraphs are present.

Regarding the fact that this issue was tested on Android with Chrome stable (v111.0.5563.57), I suggest checking if there are any specific browser-related issues that might be causing this. I recommend testing the issue on other browsers and devices to see if the issue persists.

body: From the other details you've provided, it appears that the problem is unique to Chrome for Android and might not be connected to the paragraphs' default font size. To fix this, consider increasing the font-size in the CSS rule for the card body by a few pixels over 1rem. The font-size of the text in the card-header can also be specifically changed by adding a separate CSS rule. Testing the code on several devices and browsers can help establish that the issue is unique to Chrome on Android, even though there may not be an easy fix.

author: ZhangChengLin

body:
Hello,
To make the button that jumps to the document navigation bar work on small devices, you can add a conditional statement in the HTML code that displays the button when the device width is below the minimum threshold of 768px. Regarding your request to add a button that jumps to the table of contents, you can include an anchor link in the HTML code and add a button element that links to that anchor. I suggest placing the button in the "On this page" section of the page, and setting the link's href attribute to #tocContents. This will allow users to easily navigate to the table of contents on the same page
author: mootezaloui

body:
> 

Yes, but you ignore that when the size is small, the bootstrap document navigation bar is hidden, click the jump to navigation bar button, will not trigger the display of the document navigation bar, so you should add the display of the trigger document navigation bar when you click the button, so that the focus can be positioned on the document navigation bar.
Currently, the bootstrap documentation does not do this. Instead, on small form factor devices (min-width: 768px), the jump to document navigation bar button does not appear.
author: ZhangChengLin

body:
It looks that the problem is not just with showing the button, but also with making sure that when the button is clicked on tiny devices, the document navigation bar is visible. If so, you may change the JavaScript code to include an event listener on the button element, which when clicked toggles the visibility of the document navigation bar.
For example, you could add something like this to the code: 
`document.querySelector('#jump-to-nav-button').addEventListener('click', function() {`
`  var navBar = document.querySelector('#document-nav-bar');`
` if (navBar.classList.contains('offcanvas')) {`
` navBar.classList.remove('offcanvas');`
` navBar.classList.add('show');`
`  } else {`
` navBar.classList.add('offcanvas');`
` navBar.classList.remove('show');`
`}`
`});`
This code listens for a click on the button with the ID "jump-to-nav-button", then toggles the "offcanvas" and "show" classes on the document navigation bar element with the ID "document-nav-bar". This should ensure that the navigation bar is displayed when the button is clicked on small devices. 
However You are true that the document navigation bar does not appear when the hop to navigation button is clicked on tiny form factor devices according to the current Bootstrap instructions. When the button is clicked, JavaScript may be used to alter the navigation bar's appearance in order to provide this feature. For this think this may work better 
`const jumpToNavButton = document.querySelector('#jump-to-docs-nav');`
`const navBar = document.querySelector('#bd-docs-nav');`
`jumpToNavButton.addEventListener('click', () => {`
  `if (window.innerWidth < 768) {`
   ` navBar.style.display = 'block';`
  `}`
`});`
The jumpToNavButton variable is set to the element representing a jump to navigation button, and the navBar variable is assigned to the element representing a document navigation bar. When a button is pressed, the event listener determines whether the device width is less than 768 pixels and, if it is, changes the navigation bar's display to "block" mode.

I hope this 2 solutions works for you.

body:
Hi,

I want to add a solution here, just in case others are looking for a resolution to this problem.

If you are on Windows and you edit the scss files that later will be used by Hugo to build the docs with - make sure that you save the files in Unix mode, not in DOS mode. If one of the .scss source files are in DOS mode, you will get errors like this:

ERROR "C:\Users\User\GIT\bootstrap\site\content\docs\5.3\utilities\background.md:150:1": "scss-docs": Got no matches for name="utils-bg-color" in
file="scss/_utilities.scss"!

If you have VIM installed on Windows, the solution is simple:
 - Open the file in VIM
 - Run the VIM command ':set ff=unix'
 - Save the file with ':w'

When you have fixed all the scss source files accidentally turned into DOS mode back into Unix mode, 'npm run docs-build' will work fine.

Kind regards,

/S

author: julien-deramond

body: This is basically entirely because of Sass, but we want to change this in v6. Until then, we're limited by backward compatibility, which would be broken if we suddenly stopped applying the Sass value to certain properties. In some cases we're able to reassign things to their CSS variable equivalent, but it's not a given.
author: mdo

body: Flagging that we need to test this a bit more and see if there's something to recommend for a fix here. Took me a second to understand that this was in `media-query` mode, so there's no generated data attribute for dark mode here. We may need to work around that in some way.
author: mdo

body: It seems like certain data theme attributes will still need to be generated in `media-query` mode if the intention is to use them to override the current theme color on individual components.

author: alemendoza-v

body:
This is because [the `hash` property isn't URL decoded](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Global_Objects/decodeURI). 

Using `decodeURI(anchor.hash)` in [our scrollSpy component](https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/blob/bb33b4bb8fe0d9ce52e6e2753a514700ce58f1bc/js/src/scrollspy.js#L203) should solve this (but might be used elsewhere too, as long as we use the `hash` property).
[
Check your fixed Pen](https://codepen.io/ffoodd/pen/dygGxZK) to see the difference (in the console).

body: You should avoid using components that makes direct manipulations into the DOM otherwise you're going to run into issues when using React.  You should consider using a library like `react-bootstrap` to handle this for you since the implementation is built specifically for React.

author: misiyevich

body:
The modal injects a backdrop and makes class/attribute changes.  This is considered DOM manipulation.

You really shouldn't be using jQuery alongside React due to the same reasons mentioned above (DOM manipulation).  React has its own ways of getting references to elements and you should be using that.  Also, storing a function to allow other elements to trigger the modal is not very React-like either.  You should be using props/state to do that.

body:
Hello @NateRadebaugh
You need to add `.link-underline-success` as mentioned in https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/helpers/icon-link/#utilities.
Seems to work well even with a `<button>` (see https://codepen.io/julien-deramond/pen/oNPRNEY)
author: julien-deramond

body:
Thanks that's good to know, but it seems like it'd be cleaner to have the `icon-link` respect the `link-success` directly without needing to use a separate set of utilities since I'm not trying to style the underline differently than the text itself.

Seems like `link-success` should set the right css vars by default to style the underline with "success" and then should be able to be overridden with other colors using `link-underline-*` but to *require* styling underline separately from text when there's no different color desired is unintuitive.

I guess really it's not clear why the `.icon-link` needs to set the color at all since the main purpose for this helper is to handle how the icon looks/works.

Proposals:

1. Remove `text-decoration-color` from `.icon-link`
2. Add `!important` to `.link-*` color helpers so its color applies, but then you also need `!important` on the `.link-underline-*` classes so it also gets respected.
3. Update `.link-*` and `.link-underline-*` color helpers to set the appropriate CSS variable that `.icon-link` is using, eg `--bs-link-color-rgb`
4. Keep is as is

If I were to submit a PR, I'd say implementing `3.` above would be least disruptive and continues the shift towards using CSS variables in more places.

body:
It's not a linear scale, and it never has been. The scale is as follows:

- .p-0: 0
- .p-1: .25rem
- .p-2: .5rem
- .p-3: 1rem
- .p-4: 1.5rem
- .p-5: 3rem

And to expand that to additional steps, you might have something like:

- .p-6: 4.5rem
- .p-7: 6rem

Or you might have something like:

- .p-6: 6rem
- .p-7: 9rem

That all depends on how you want to scale the spacing. The original idea here is that you usually need a range of tighter spaces more often, and then even more options for creating white space at large sizes. Like having a 80px font size with 4px of padding isn't right, you need something to match the scale of that text.

body:
My personal opinion is that it could be very useful to have this kind of tool for Bootstrap for obvious reasons.

But to have that, I suppose that our documentation should use Prettier in the first place, otherwise it doesn't mean anything (to avoid "we provide something we don't even use"). And then, we would have to build and maintain the tool.
So, on my side, I'm far from being against it. But IMO we don't have right now enough resources to do it because of other priorities within the framework itself. So I would tend to add a `help-wanted` label for this issue and wait for the help of someone in the community.

I'll let others on the team provide their thoughts.

body:
hello, @realthemes,
the "flex-nowrap" class on a tag that causes a horizontal scrollbar to show up in a column that is nested inside of a row. By including "style=flex: 1; min-width: 0;"  in the col element, you have discovered a solution. You did note that this problem extends beyond the tag and impacts nested Prism JS code blocks. The code block can fit in the container generally if the "flex-nowrap" class isn't present. It's significant to note that you are using Bootstrap v5.3.0 alpha1 and that this problem is occurring on Google Chrome.
Another solution is  The "overflow-x: auto;" feature is added to the container by adding a "flex-nowrap" class tag within a flex container. The code blocks will fit appropriately within the container without requiring any additional CSS styles thanks to the limited use of horizontal scrolling. This technique might not be practical, though, if you wish to keep the "flex-nowrap" class for other components in the container.

body: @mootezaloui, thanks for the message, [here](https://weblog.west-wind.com/posts/2016/feb/15/flexbox-containers-pre-tags-and-managing-overflow) is a detailed description of the problem and its solution, it seems that the problem is not in the bootstrap, but in particular the work of flex containers
author: realthemes

body: Same as the general `.col` overflow behavior: a flex column without `min-width: 0` lets wide `<pre>` content overflow — standard flexbox, fixable with `min-width: 0`/`overflow` on the column. Closing as by-design — please open a new issue if you see a regression in v6-dev.

@ryanwawr One thing that might be worth trying is to add the "data-boundary" property to the dropdown button element and set it to "viewport". This will constrain the dropdown menu to the bounds of the viewport, which could help prevent it from getting stuck in an open or closed state. If that doesn't work, you could also try using a different approach to handling dropdown menus, such as using a custom JavaScript implementation instead of relying on Bootstrap's built-in functionality. `

body:
> This is covered in our docs—we have new .text-body-* classes to do color mode friendly text colors.

This is but still, https://twbs-bootstrap.netlify.app/docs/5.3/components/navbar/#external-content is broken in light mode: the "Toggleable via the navbar brand." text is not visible.

Regarding this issue, in https://twbs-bootstrap.netlify.app/docs/5.3/components/navbar/#color-schemes we are using `data-bs-theme="dark"` in combination with `.bg-dark`.
So in https://twbs-bootstrap.netlify.app/docs/5.3/components/navbar/#external-content, in order to display correctly "Toggleable via the navbar brand." in all cases, we could use `data-bs-theme="dark"` as well since `.bg-dark` is forced for this example in light and dark mode.
No need to change the rest of the content nor to reintegrate `.text-muted` which we should keep deprecated.
**Edit:** See https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/pull/38431

Another possibility would be to drop this use of `.bg-dark` here which is not very useful since we are talking here about "External content" and not the colors. And so prefer the usage of `.bg-body-tertiary` (like the other examples on this page).

body:
We have a lot of custom properties not scoped to the top-level `:root` element. For example:

https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/blob/b000f2ace1cd70a141ff0a90f2d18a57d4ecfae4/dist/css/bootstrap.css#L738

It is voluntary.

So the warnings should be disabled in this precise use case in your PostCSS configuration (if I understand correctly the screenshot, they are generated by postcss-loader).
I've seen some things like https://github.com/MoOx/postcss-cssnext/issues/186 that could give you an idea of how to disable those warnings.

So I'm closing the issue since it's not an issue on Bootstrap side.

body: v6-dev rebuilds offcanvas as a `drawer` on the native `<dialog>` element and no longer sets `aria-modal` itself — modality now comes from `showModal()` vs `show()`. A drawer with `backdrop: false` + `scroll: true` opens non-modal, so the content underneath stays accessible and the mismatch this describes is gone. Closing — please open a new issue if it persists in v6-dev.

body: Most of these are because we have `null` value Sass variables. This is especially true for `color` and `font-size` values where we assume an inherited value. The breadcrumb divider and nav max-height are there as optional override variables, so there's no default assigned value needed.

body:
Reacting to https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/pull/38489#issuecomment-1518506999:

> Let's keep the PRs at bay for now and discuss any potential changes in the issue. I could see us darkening things a little, but this is too much

I think this regression happened in [fc3f4b67](https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/commit/fc3f4b67d65c575daa661ecf31cf59b4ff3cced5#diff-108a57a50d5ebe203f447ef7bf1e5e53826bdb82baab2baa2b41b4158cdd4d09R873):

- `$input-border-color` was changed from `$gray-400` to `var(--border-color)` which defaults to `$gray-300`
- the color in `$form-check-input-border` was changed from `rgba($black, .25)` also to `var(--#{$prefix}border-color)`

I see two options to fix this:

- Add a new CSS variable `--input-border-color` and set it to `$gray-400`
- Change `--border-color` to `$gray-400`

@mdo which option do you prefer?

Btw, I also checked the contrast for dark mode ([`#495057` on `#212529`](https://contrast-ratio.com/#%23495057-on-%23212529)) and it fares much better: 1.88 is still too low, but much better than the contrasts in light mode.

body: It sounds like you are encountering an issue where you are unable to focus on an input field inside a Toast component when a Modal is displayed on the screen. This is a common problem that occurs when there are multiple layers of UI components on the screen, and the Modal is taking focus away from the Toast. One solution to this problem is to use a higher z-index value for the Toast component, which will ensure that it is displayed on top of the Modal. Additionally, you can add a tabindex attribute to the input field inside the Toast, which will allow users to focus on the input field using the keyboard.

body:
I'm trying a first quick analysis here.

IMO this behavior is linked to the focus trap system in place for modals. When a modal is displayed, when navigating with your keyboard, you can't focus on anything else on the page until you close this modal. We could argue that the focus trap could allow exceptions, but I'd say that if you would use a `<dialog>` instead of a modal from Bootstrap, you would have the same behavior here, and you probably wouldn't be able to change it because it's handled by browsers directly.

Based on the CodePen, I'd also say that we shouldn't even be able to click on the toast buttons while the modal is displayed.

Thoughts @twbs/js-review?
@patrickhlauke Do you have specific elements on the a11y side to add or precise here?

author: mdo

body:
The issue with table-striped overwriting table-active in Bootstrap can be resolved by increasing the specificity of the CSS selector for the table-active class.
One way to do this is to add a parent selector to the table-active class, which will make it more specific than the table-striped class. For example, you can add a class to the parent element of the table, and use that class as a selector for the table-active class.
Here is an example CSS code that shows how this can be done:

.my-table .table-active {
  background-color: #f5c6cb;
}
In this code, .my-table is a parent class that is added to the parent element of the table. This makes the selector more specific than the .table-striped class, so the background color of the table-active elements will not be overwritten by the striping.
You can adjust the background-color value to the desired color for your active table rows.
By using a more specific selector, you can ensure that the active color is applied to every table row that has the table-active class, regardless of whether the table is striped or not.
I can take this issue to fix it 
Hope i could help

body:
You appear to be making an effort to support the creation of the Offcanvas component inside the Bootstrap open source project. In the problem you brought up, it is suggested that support be added for several Offcanvas sizes, such "offcanvas-size-sm," "offcanvas-size-lg," "offcanvas-size-xl," etc.

The existing Offcanvas code could be changed to support these new size classes and the Offcanvas width could be adjusted as a result. To support the "offcanvas-size-lg" class, you might edit the Offcanvas code as shown in the following example:


`.offcanvas-size-lg {`
  `width: 500px; /* Change the width to 500px */`
`}`

`.offcanvas.show.offcanvas-size-lg {`
 ` transform: translateX(0); /* Reset the transform property */`
`}`




For the other size classes, you can also apply equivalent CSS rules. The JavaScript code for the Offcanvas must also be changed in order to handle these new size classes and toggle the proper class when the Offcanvas is opened.

Before sending a pull request, don't forget to test your changes properly and make sure they work on all popular browsers and gadgets.

author: ffoodd

body:
Thank you for bringing highlighting this issue. I was able to reproduce the problem on different browsers and operating systems as described. It seems that the nav-link tabs with data-bs-toggle="tab" prevent the default browser behavior for the alt + left/right arrow keys, which is the expected behavior for navigating browser history.
One possible solution could be to add a condition to the keydown event listener for the nav-link tabs that checks for the alt key and allows the default browser behavior to continue if it is pressed. Another option could be to provide a way for users to disable this behavior if they wish.

body:
Adjusting the CSS property "--bs-alert-padding-y " can affect the vertical padding of the alert, but it may cause odd display effects when using a dismiss button because the button's position is also affected by the padding.
In order to address this issue, you may want to consider adjusting the padding of the alert container instead of the alert itself. This way, the dismiss button will not be affected, and the alert will still have the desired amount of padding.
For example, instead of using --bs-alert-padding-y, you can try using --bs-alert-container-padding-y to adjust the padding of the alert container. This should allow you to control the spacing around the alert while avoiding any odd display effects with the dismiss button.
Alternatively, you can try adjusting the position of the dismiss button using CSS, so it remains in the correct position even with adjusted padding. For example, you can use position: absolute and top, bottom, left or right properties to position the dismiss button. However, this approach requires some additional CSS code and may not be the most efficient solution.

Breakpoints don’t specifically target every use case or device so `xxl` (`≥1400px`) might not fit the exact values needed for iMac and TV or extra Large Monitors.

body:
There is a static [method](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/components/modal/#methods) `getInstance` which allows you to get the modal instance associated with a DOM element. If you change the line `let bootstrapModal = new bootstrap.Modal(modal);` from your example to `let bootstrapModal = bootstrap.Modal.getInstance(modal);`, your example is working and the modal closes.

(There is also a static `getOrCreateInstance` method in case you cannot ensure that the modal has already been initialised by the call via the button)

body:
Such renaming and breaking changes only happens in major releases, following semantic versioning conventions.

Major releases imply breaking changes.

However minor and patch releases should not contain any breaking change, so yes, Bootstrap tends to be backwards compatible.

Adding onto what @ffoodd said, yes, there is a reason. We added RTL support, which changed how we think of directions for page layout and more. We were quite up front about this in the move from v4 to v5 in our development and blog posts :).

Haven't tested it and IDK if it's something we can actually do; but maybe we could consider moving the content of `_variables-dark.scss` at the end of `_variables.scss` (since the Sass var names are all different) surrounded by a `@if $enable-dark-mode`  in a v5.3.1 so that there's no need to import a new file while migrating. And reintroduce this `_variables-dark.scss` only for v6.

One other option would be to import `_variables-dark.scss` at the end of our `_variables.scss`. Not very consistent with the way we handle Sass files currently we could allow us to keep separate files and limit the breaking change.
_(Haven't tried it)_

@twbs/css-review thoughts?

body:
ok, if the bootstrap team disagrees and decides to force the people to update their code, that's fine, it's a very easy fix, but don't deny the facts...
 
In some circumstances, the 5.3.0 update breaks projects running 5.2.3... so yes, it's a **Breaking Change** because the update 5.3.0 is not safe to apply... a minor change requirement is broken... The Backwards Compatibility.

References:
- "Backward compatible new features" https://docs.npmjs.com/about-semantic-versioning

- "MINOR version when you add functionality in a backward compatible manner" https://semver.org/#semantic-versioning-200

body:
> Minor updates have always allowed for new features, and most new features come with new files (e.g., adding toasts). I think having these new ones as separate stylesheets is fine. Maybe there's more we can do to document the migration?

The difference here is that the end user can opt into toasts, and not having toasts enabled does not break existing builds. This update broke existing builds, and therefore is a breaking change. It's not about adding the feature, it's about the implementation.

To reiterate what was already said - the fix was very straightforward. But shouldn't have been necessary at all.

body:
> > I've had the same issue stemming from an update in [bootstrap-vue-next/bootstrap-vue-next@main...VividLemon:bootstrap-vue-next:main#diff-e04aecfce6b6cb542767b096855fc5eb2681ba5a764f65167447440a9df1a92eR66](https://github.com/bootstrap-vue-next/bootstrap-vue-next/compare/main...VividLemon:bootstrap-vue-next:main#diff-e04aecfce6b6cb542767b096855fc5eb2681ba5a764f65167447440a9df1a92eR66) @ https://github.com/VividLemon/bootstrap-vue-next/blob/main/packages/bootstrap-vue-next/src/components/BToast/_toast.scss#L1 Which is odd because it exists in https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/components/toasts/#sass-variables And even odder as my import of scss and the Vue compiler didn't change. I may try downgrading to see the behavior, but at the moment I have more pressing issues in other dependencies.
> 
> In v5.2.3, here was the definition of this variable:
> 
> https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/blob/cb021439c683d9805e2864c58095b92d405e9b11/scss/_variables.scss#L1370
> 
> In v5.3.0, it is now:
> 
> https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/blob/60098ac499d30aa50575b0b7137391c06ef25429/scss/_variables.scss#L1472
> 
> In your source code, there's `b-toast-background-opacity: alpha($toast-background-color) !default;`.
> 
> The change of definition of `$toast-background-color` using custom properties now breaks Sass compilation when called with `alpha`, and would also fail with `color-contrast` Bootstrap's function because of `contrast-ratio` called within this function.
> 
> It can be tested easily with:
> 
> ```css-scss
> $test: rgba($white, 0.85);
> @debug(alpha($test));
> @debug(alpha($toast-background-color)); // Only this one fails during Sass compilation
> ```
> 
> This is a first quick analysis, but Bootstrap's dark mode implementation relies a lot on custom properties in `_variables.scss`, so I'm afraid that depending on the users, especially advanced Sass users, some calls won't work as it was the case before resulting in Sass compilation errors.

Do you perhaps have a temporary fix solution that I can use while I try to comprehend the purpose of the custom scss?

body:
I had similar issue after updating from Bootstrap 5.2.3 to 5.3.0.

It turned out that I had leftover, no longer existing element in the `data-bs-target` attribute.

```html
<button class="navbar-toggler" data-bs-target="#top-navbar-collapse, aside">
</button>
```
`<aside>` element has been removed a while ago, but navigation bar collapse functionality continued to work in the version of Bootstrap lower than `5.3`. Navbar collapse in version `5.3` does not working until the extra element reference has been removed from the  `data-bs-target` attribute.

```html
<button class="navbar-toggler" data-bs-target="#top-navbar-collapse">
</button>
```

body:
Thanks for reporting his issue @svdm.
Indeed, it's in the same spirit as described in https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/issues/38687#issuecomment-1575194577.

```css
--test: #fd0;
background: darken(var(--test), 20%);
```

Generates a compilation error:
```
Error: $color: var(--test) is not a color.
   ╷
79 │   background: darken(var(--test), 20%);
   │               ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
```

Back to your example, `$card-bg` is now set in v5.3.0 to  `var(--bs-body-bg)` and was set to `$white` in v5.2.3.
That's why the error now pops up. Mostly because Sass color functions don't work with custom properties as parameters defining a color.

body:
I almost created a PR, but realized I actually want it inverted for `btn-outline-subtle`. That is how my custom color modes are before 5.3. Has anyone else thought of this? `btn-outline-light` is almost invisible (and useless) in light mode, while `btn-outline-dark` is completely invisible in dark mode. 

In effect:
* In light mode, `btn-outline-subtle` would be the same as `btn-outline-dark`
* In dark mode, `btn-outline-subtle` would be the same as `btn-outline-light`

This is probably confusing and too inconsistent compared to other `*-subtle` components. 

Perhaps subtle outline buttons aren't needed, but instead we could have another variant `btn[-outline]-{contrast,inverted,monochrome}` or similar.

body:
> > Anyway I don't think that's an issue from Bootstrap.
> 
> I agree with @CyrilKrylatov. From what I understand, I don't think that's an issue from Bootstrap since the `<iframe>` is independent of Bootstrap's dark mode defined in the "main" content. So closing it.

Yes and no. It's true that styles defined in Bootstrap aren't applied inside the `iframe`, but the `color-scheme` property, if set, does get passed to the `iframe`, which affects how it's displayed. See [this CodePen](https://codepen.io/dougwaldron/pen/GRzWKNG) for examples.

It seems to me that Bootstrap should set `iframe { color-scheme: normal; }` but that would be a breaking change I guess.

body: The white iframe background comes from the embedded third-party content's own `color-scheme`/background — Bootstrap can't style cross-origin iframe internals. Closing as out of scope — please open a new issue if you find a Bootstrap-side cause in v6-dev.
author: mdo

body:
> The white iframe background comes from the embedded third-party content's own `color-scheme`/background — Bootstrap can't style cross-origin iframe internals. Closing as out of scope — please open a new issue if you find a Bootstrap-side cause in v6-dev.

Nope. It comes from browser quirks around the css `color-scheme` set on the host document.  Iframes default to inheriting the host document here
author: androidacy-user

@mdo This issue contains multiple examples showing that the `color-scheme` setting on the parent document does indeed affect how the `iframe` is displayed.
author: dougwaldron

body:
Sorry, I've been quickly triaging in bulk.

Okay so this is a weird one. Did some research and here's the CSS working group explanation for the issue:

> If the color scheme of an iframe differs from embedding document, iframe gets an opaque canvas background appropriate to its color scheme.
> 
> https://fvsch.com/transparent-iframes

So any mismatch results in a browser-set background.

I think setting the following should suffice, and be relatively bulletproof for us without causing more downstream issues, being placed inside the reboot stylesheet?

```css
iframe {
  color-scheme: light dark;
}
```
author: mdo

body:
> I think setting the following should suffice, and be relatively bulletproof for us without causing more downstream issues, being placed inside the reboot stylesheet?
> 
> iframe {
>   color-scheme: light dark;
> }

@mdo Thanks for reopening this! And good find with that article.

I put together a new CodePen that tests multiple scenarios here: https://codepen.io/dougwaldron/pen/KwaXoxY (Don't forget to test the page with your OS set to both light and dark mode and reload the page after changing!)


In the CodePen, I set the `color-scheme` of the parent element to light, dark, or not set, and did the same for the content inside the `iframe` (modeling what would come from a third-party component). Then I set the `color-scheme` of the `iframe` element itself to various values to model what could be set by Bootstrap. These include:

* no `color-scheme` set (current behavior)
* `normal`
* `light`
* `dark`
* `light dark`
* `normal light dark`
* `unset`

Unfortunately, your proposed fix (`light dark`) doesn't seem to work in all situations, particularly when the OS is set to dark mode, but I would appreciate someone double checking my work.

author: habibfyssal-bit

body: > It looks correct here for me? https://v6-dev--twbs-bootstrap.netlify.app/docs/6.0/content/reboot/ > > Or is there another combination this doesn't account for? In your examples, the content in each `iframe` sets its own color scheme to `light dark` using ``. But if your `iframe` is embedding a third-party component (e.g., Paypal, TrustPilot), I'd guess that more often than not, that component won't have a color scheme set at all. When the OS or browser is set to dark mode, and the `iframe` element has `color-scheme: light dark`, then the content will have an opaque white background (just as it does in Bootstrap v5): I'd suggest using `iframe { color-scheme: normal; }` instead. In all cases where no color scheme is set by the embedded content, the `iframe` renders with a transparent background. The one consideration with using `color-scheme: normal` is that an `iframe` with dark-mode content will render with an opaque black background (even if the page/parent element is also in dark mode). But I'd argue that if the content is setting it's own color scheme to dark mode, then it should be setting its own background color as well, so the `iframe` background won't show anyway.

body: As far as I understand the [button documentation for disabled state](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/components/buttons/#disabled-state) the class `disabled` is only a workaround for `a`nchors, because they ignore the `disabled` attribute. So `button`s should be fine without the class. It's also written there that `aria-disabled="true"` should be set for these disabled anchors, which is also not the case for the examples in both Navs & tabs and Navbar.

body:
Hey, thanks for raising the issue.

For now I think it's safer to keep the selector `.table > :not(caption) > * > *` to handle correctly the nesting and don't break anything for people that might need those selector. IMHO, it could be rethought sooner or later to make it less selective since we have CSS variables now. I'll try to think about it, and if possible as a non-breaking change for folks so that could be embedded asap otherwise, it might happen for v6.

However, I think that this subject is unrelated to that issue because as you said it used to work in previous Bootstrap versions.

I don't know your specific use case but I managed to tweak a bit the code for now to be merged as soon as possible in the linked PR. I've updated the linked PR to make this use case work without breaking anything else. Could you please check that the fix work with your use case ?

If not, feel free to open a PR and we'll discuss about your solutions.

body:
Thanks for highlighting the issue with a picture. I've been setting up several pages now, still with 5.3.0-alpha3 until this has been sorted out - or eventually move to something else altogether.

>and don't break anything for people that might need those selector.

This statement feels wrong to me - this has been a breaking change in behaviour compared to previous versions, yet it is now being kept around in case people rely on it? What about all the developers and cases where the old behaviour, i.e. no behaviour, was intended?

If it were at least possible to turn this behaviour off with a variable or similar, it would be more bearable, but as far as I know, that's not really possible?

body: I _think_ this broke starting in v5.3.0, and it's definitely still there in v5.3.3. The selector `.table > :not(caption) > * > *` is just too greedy. The same problem happens if you apply `bg-warning` to a `tr`, because the child `td` will get this rule applied. This breaks something you could do going back to very early BS versions.

body:
Guys this is a huge issue, I can't believe this is unresolved since 2023. 

I've used Bootstrap 5.3.3 for a new project and I found myself adding more and more hacks because on a dark design, I would get random blotches of white background in a table. 

I had to downgrade to Bootstrap 5.2, which immediately fixed this, and allowed me to remove all these hacks. When multiple people cry "this needs to be fixed soon" and weeks pass, and people revert to using alphas (or downgrading), then something's amiss.
author: jackie-t

body: It's not encouraging that it's marked as "nice to have" for v5.3.4. This breaks tables so badly.
author: jeffputz

body:
> Guys this is a huge issue, I can't believe this is unresolved since 2023.

> It's not encouraging that it's marked as "nice to have" for v5.3.4. This breaks tables so badly.

It's listed as 'nice to have' for v5.3.4 since the focus of this release was elsewhere, and it's already slightly delayed for various reasons. However, that doesn't mean it won't make it into v5.3.5, for example. Just keep in mind, Bootstrap is maintained by volunteers in their spare time. We're doing our best with the limited hours we have. :)

Feel free to help review https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/pull/38826 by thoroughly testing it to ensure we don't fix one issue only to break something else. That's the challenge we're facing with the table colors: we fix one thing, and while some folks are happy, it ends up causing issues in other unexpected use cases. The use cases are numerous depending on how the markup is used, so it can be a little bit tricky to handle everything.
author: julien-deramond

body: I appreciate the volunteer efforts, I just feel like 8 months is a long time to go between releases. As it stands now, the v5.3.4 only has effectively two "mandatory" items in it, which seems pretty low risk in terms of adding other things.
author: jeffputz

body:
Likewise, I appreciate the volunteer efforts. You should read my comment as "watch out, this is a huge issue that undermines your efforts of making great software". All the other nice things that may be added aren't useful, if I can't use bootstrap due to how it breaks tables.

This shouldn't downplay your efforts, just alert you that this is something big that overshadows them, and that appears getting missed (due to how much this has been open).

This warning is displayed as soon as one is clicking on the touch simulation button in Firefox. On the right-hand side of the warning, the corresponding file "generating" the warning is mentioned: here it's `view-source:resource://devtools/server/actors/emulation/touch-simulator.js`. This file doesn't come from Bootstrap, so it's not an issue on our side, and probably on Firefox side.

body:
https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/pull/38845#pullrequestreview-1504675789 explains why we can't use `.overflow-hidden` in our example because it's not a generic functional approach; if one uses dropdowns for example, it won't work.

As a user, using CSS or generating something like `.rounded-md-start` would do the trick. But here, in our documentation, we can't do that.

One solution could be to force the horizontal layout whatever the breakpoint and change the following explanation:

> In the example below, we remove the grid gutters with .g-0 and use .col-md-* classes to make the card horizontal at the md breakpoint. Further adjustments may be needed depending on your card content.

Other ideas?

> Could be a use case for fallback value in var(), couldn't it?

I haven't checked the specific use case of this issue yet but it could yeah.

More globally in the framework, I'm wondering if we shouldn't define `color: var(--bs-body-color)` at the root level in dark mode (which I think is not the case right now). It would probably allow us to keep the inheritance of the basic color most of the time instead of redefining it for each component (it wasn't the case before the dark mode). Not sure that my quick explanation is understandable, but I'll take some time to test it.

body: Hello @HybridSolutions ! By default tooltip element has a `tooltip` class, I don't think that more default classes are required. You can change `max-width` value by passing your own class to the tooltip component (you can also use this class for several tooltips if needed) and then define CSS custom property `--#{$prefix}tooltip-max-width: #{$tooltip-max-width};` ```HTML ``` ```CSS .html-tooltip { --bs-tooltip-max-width: 400px; } ```
author: niktariy

body: can you assign me the task of adding css class for tooltip html mode
author: Jyoticharan

body: > Hello @HybridSolutions ! By default tooltip element has a `tooltip` class, I don't think that more default classes are required. You can change `max-width` value by passing your own class to the tooltip component (you can also use this class for several tooltips if needed) and then define CSS custom property `--#{$prefix}tooltip-max-width: #{$tooltip-max-width};` > > ``` > > ``` > > ```css > .html-tooltip { > --bs-tooltip-max-width: 400px; > } > ``` I'm aware of the alternative. I only made the suggestion because the class `bs-tooltip-auto` is being placed when attribute `data-bs-placement="auto"` is used, so, to be coherent, it would make sense to also add a `bs-tooltip-html` when using `data-bs-html="true"`. Cheers ;)
author: HybridSolutions
msathyaanand

body:
> Find the css code > > `.bs-tooltip-html { max-width: none; } ` Please check the js code for the same > > ``` > $(function () { > $('[data-toggle="tooltip"][data-html="true"]').tooltip({ > template: '
' > }); > }); > ``` > > This code adds a new CSS class called bs-tooltip-html that removes the max-width limit from tooltips. It also adds a new option to the tooltip initialization that checks for the data-html attribute and sets the template option accordingly, adding the bs-tooltip-html class to the tooltip element. With this code, you can now use the data-html="true" attribute on your tooltips to enable HTML content and extend the max-width if needed. Thanks for the effort. I know all these workarounds but the issue I was pointing was not how to find a way of changing the CSS of an html tooltip, but a design issue. BS is assigning other classes according to attributes so it would make sense to also have the class i mentioned assigned as well. Both the custom class or this workaround works but the core design is not correct. Html tooltips should not have max-width set to a fixed value by default. It should have the `“fit-content”` value. I understand if you don't agree with my approach. I just decided to give my opinion on this matter ;)
author: HybridSolutions

body: I don't mind adding a `.bs-tooltip-html` when `data-bs-html="true"` for consistency. @GeoSot what do you think?
author: julien-deramond

body:
> “fit-content”

It's not just that. Max-width should be set to “fit-content” when `data-bs-html` attribute is set to `true`.
author: HybridSolutions

body:
> I don't mind adding a `.bs-tooltip-html` when `data-bs-html="true"` for consistency. @GeoSot what do you think?

Seems a simple & fair add-on, but can easily be covered by the solution @niktariy [suggested](https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/issues/38874#issuecomment-1630160688) too, above
author: GeoSot

body: Rather than auto-adding a class for HTML tooltips, use `customClass` (`data-bs-custom-class`) to target them, together with the `--tooltip-max-width` custom property for sizing. Closing as working-as-intended — please open a new issue if that approach falls short in v6-dev.

> Thanks for submitting this issue @vovochka-dev. I'm not sure to understand what's the issue here. Are you using the buttons without Bootstrap CSS?

I am not using the buttons without Bootstrap CSS , I want use default unstyled button element with  Bootstrap CSS. But  Bootstrap CSS change default button element  behaviour. 

One of the reasons Bootstrap uses classes like .btn instead of applying styles directly to the button element itself is to allow for flexibility and compatibility with various scenarios and customization needs.

By using classes to apply styles, Bootstrap maintains a separation between the structural HTML elements and the visual styling applied to them. This allows users to easily customize the appearance of elements while keeping the core HTML structure intact.

In scenarios where third-party libraries or content WYSIWYG editors are used within a Bootstrap layout, it's important to preserve the basic characteristics of HTML elements, including the focus outline. This ensures that the accessibility and usability of the elements are not compromised.

By following best practices and not overly modifying the essential characteristics of HTML elements, such as the focus outline, you can maintain compatibility with external components, improve accessibility, and make it easier to work with different tools and editors.

I have dug further into this and it looks like bootstraps `defineJQueryPlugin(Popover)` and its containing `onDOMContentLoaded` is getting called before the `window.jQuery = jQuery` line in `main.js`. So `window.jQuery` is not defined at the time it is trying to register the plugin.

I guess this is something to do with the way Vite loads dependencies

body:
This is actually really easy to fix, it's just not documented anywhere.

Create a "wrapper" for jquery like this:

`jquery_import.js`

```
import $ from 'jquery';
window.$ = window.jQuery = $;
```

In your main js file, import this wrapper before importing the JS for bootstrap 5.

If you don't use a wrapper file, Vite moves the global declaration to the very end of the built JS file, and it won't work.

If I were to create a PR for this, what should the API be? `bootstrap.setjQuery(...)`  would match the internal `getjQuery`, but there are no other utility methods exported by Bootstrap at this time - you might want to avoid such a thing? It should also be noted a new method would only be relevant if called prior to `DOMContentLoaded` being triggered, as that [is when](https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/blob/5210c9b24c44f8230c3ca4813d30211289f27b2e/js/src/util/index.js#L208) the jQuery plugins are set (I support it would be possible to recall that function when `setjQuery` is called, but why would one ever want to change jQuery version after load...?).

author: mdo

body:
@mdo The "starter project" is only used in the repo instructions, this is a general issue. It appears that the bootstrap nuget package works as intended with ASP.NET 4.8 projects, but not with ASP.NET 5+ (aka Core) projects.

This issue has also been documented in quite a few SO questions, all of which have the "solution" of not using nuget:

https://stackoverflow.com/q/76673639/87698
https://stackoverflow.com/q/68353885/87698
https://stackoverflow.com/q/57038463/87698

Apparently, in ASP.NET 5+ projects, the relevant files are just linked instead of copied to the project. If you copy the files manually, you lose the ability to manage your dependency with nuget, i.e., upgrading the nuget package will (obviously) not upgrade the files you copied manually into your project.

Unfortunately, I am only familiar with consuming nuget packages, not with writing them, so I don't know whether this can be fixed. If it can't be fixed (which would be a pity, since it's *extremely* convenient to have all .NET dependencies managed by the same system), it would be nice to somehow mark the package as "not compatible with .NET 5+".

author: thatcode

body:
(I noticed my code had div's in the order container-row-row. I've now removed the outer row but it still shows up!)

I *think* I've worked this out. And there's likely nothing you can do that would work on all browsers.

XS
- We use 100% of the screen width, so everything is always squashed right next to each other. So no problem.

SM
- Viewport Width is 540px
- Container has 12px padding, so is 516px wide
- Row has 2px border, so is 512px wide.
- Col has 2px border, so is 69 1/7px wide, which browser calls 69.141px.
- Note that 0.141*7 = 0.987, just less than a whole number.
- Problem is seen when browser width is odd.

MD
- Viewport Width is 720px
- Container has 12px padding, so is 696px wide
- Row has 2px border, so is 692px wide.
- Col has 2px border, so is 94 6/7px wide, which browser calls 94.859px.
- Note that 0.859*7 = 6.013, just more than a whole number.
- Problem is not seen.

LG
- Viewport Width is 960px
- Container has 12px padding, so is 936px wide
- Row has 2px border, so is 932px wide.
- Col has 2px border, so is 129 1/7px wide, which browser calls 129.141px.
- Note that 0.141*7 = 0.987, just less than a whole number.
- Problem is seen when browser width is odd.

XL
- Viewport Width is 1140px
- Container has 12px padding, so is 1116px wide
- Row has 2px border, so is 1112px wide.
- Col has 2px border, so is 154 6/7px wide, which browser calls 154.859px.
- Note that 0.859*7 = 6.013, just more than a whole number.
- Problem is not seen.

XXL
- Viewport Width is 1320px
- Container has 12px padding, so is 1296px wide
- Row has 2px border, so is 1292px wide.
- Col has 2px border, so is 180 4/7px wide, which browser calls 180.578px.
- Note that 0.578*7 = 4.046, just more than a whole number.
- Problem is not seen.

So we see the problem when the browser width is odd and the residual column width, across all 7 columns, is just less than a whole number. Here's my theory:

1) When the browser width is odd, it has an odd number of pixels to divide over the two sides. It decides to reduce the pixel width on the right hand side.
2) This pulls the border on the row a pixel to the right.
3) When the residual is just *over* a whole number, it goes with.
4) When the residual is just *less than* a whole number, it doesn't - and a gap is seen.

If I'm right:
1) I'm not quite sure where this bug is, or how best for you to fix this for all number of columns. It'll likely show up differently on e.g., 11 columns.
2) I've no idea how Edge works out these widths. I note that 1/7 is 0.1428, rounded to 0.143, so Edge calling it 0.141 is wild...
3) I can likely work around this by strategically adding padding to the row on different viewport sizes so that the rounding is always in my favour. [EDIT: I've now confirmed this workaround - it works]

Up to you what you do with this issue - if you think you can help the browser do better I'm happy to keep it open. If you think it's beyond what Bootstrap can do, I'm happy for you to close it :)

body:
Thanks for reporting this issue @VividLemon 

Our migration guide mentions which `*-dark` are deprecated (source: https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/migration/#color-modes-1):

> **Deprecated** Color modes replace dark variants for components, so `.btn-close-white`, `.carousel-dark`, `.dropdown-menu-dark`, and `.navbar-dark` are deprecated.

For each variant, normally, you should have a warning callout like this one https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/components/dropdowns/#dark-dropdowns explaining if the dark variant is deprecated.

For the table variants, there's an info callout in https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/content/tables/#variants:

> **Heads up!** Because of the more complicated CSS used to generate our table variants, they most likely won’t see color mode adaptive styling until v6. 

However, I can easily understand that it's not that clear since v5.3 is the first step toward better color modes integration.

IDK if it brings more clarity.
Maybe you have some ideas on how and where to explain it better in the documentation?

author: julien-deramond

body:
@julien-deramond The problem with your solution is that it breaks intended light/dark mode behavior. `<html data-bs-theme="dark">` can no longer affect the dropdown on which you put 'data-bs-theme="light"' attribute.

I am not sure if codepen examples can attach HTML attribute to `<html>` tag, so it is hard to show that.

Original approach with `navbar-dark` leaves dropdown "alone", so it can follow color scheme of light/dark mode, like any other dropdown outside of the navbar.

**Update**

I managed to toggle `data-bs-theme` attribute via button and some JavaScript. I updated my original codepen samples and also extended your example, so it is clear what is going on:
https://codepen.io/nenadvicentic/pen/rNQqbxp
author: nenadvicentic
julien-deramond

body:
@julien-deramond Thank you for this very useful and clear reply. Yes, you hit my use case straight to the point.

I think the main confusion comes from the fact that two different concepts of "dark" are now mixed together. From my point of view `navbar-dark` was doing something useful and different from what `dark-mode` does.

It is easiest to explain on buttons example from the documentation:
https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/components/buttons/#variants

In the auto/light mode and Bootstrap versions preceding 5.3, they look like this:
![image](https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/assets/4405210/3ede7363-1785-494e-88d1-4154daa55172)

Text color in buttons switches from white to black, calculated based on `$min-contrast-ratio` SCSS variable value. So lighter the background becomes, text eventually switches from white to black.

If we switch page to the `dark` mode, correctly, none of the buttons text color changes:
![image](https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/assets/4405210/1304674f-9158-43f5-9571-5e66405d15e9)

`btn-primary` keeps it's white color, because background is blue. Contrast is preserved.

We both wanted do achieve same behavior with a navbar. When we assign color to a navbar, for example `bg-primary`, to have a class that will "tell" to navbar have same behavior as a button, (while nested dropdowns do change with a color mode settings).

So, how I see this, `navbar-dark` was telling "use bright text color because I am dark". Perhaps more general class could be added named something like `text-contrast` and made generally applicable to any component?

```html
<nav class="navbar bg-primary text-contrast" aria-label="Top Navigation Bar">
</nav>
<nav class="navbar bg-secondary text-contrast" aria-label="Top Navigation Bar">
</nav>
<nav class="navbar bg-warning text-contrast" aria-label="Top Navigation Bar">
</nav>
<nav class="navbar bg-info text-contrast" aria-label="Top Navigation Bar">
</nav>
```

If this is not feasible perhaps simpler to understand option would be to introduce `btn-text-primary` and similar classes:

```html
<nav class="navbar bg-primary btn-text-primary" aria-label="Top Navigation Bar">
</nav>
<nav class="navbar bg-secondary btn-text-secondary " aria-label="Top Navigation Bar">
</nav>
<nav class="navbar bg-warning btn-text-warning " aria-label="Top Navigation Bar">
</nav>
<nav class="navbar bg-info btn-text-info " aria-label="Top Navigation Bar">
</nav>
```

From my point of view, it is important not to mix concepts of text contrast and light/dark modes. And I think that recommending to use `data-bs-theme` attributes on different elements inside of a page to achieve certain color scheme or contrast tends to blur this two concepts.

body: v6-dev removes `.navbar-dark` and reworks the navbar onto theme tokens — a dark navbar is `bg-primary` + `data-bs-theme="dark"`, and inner dropdowns can be scoped with their own `data-bs-theme`. The old divergence no longer applies. Closing as resolved in v6-dev — please open a new issue if it persists there.

body:
In versions 5.3.0 and 5.3.1, there was a change in the behavior of the directional "border-bottom" property when applied in combination with other directional borders (e.g., "border-top," "border-left," "border-right"). This change caused the directional "border-bottom" to stop functioning as expected in some cases. To demonstrate this issue, here's an example code snippet for a table with CSS styles applied: HTML: ```html
Cell 1 Cell 2
Cell 3 Cell 4
``` CSS (version 5.3.1 and earlier): ``` .my-table td { border-top: 1px solid black; border-left: 1px solid black; border-right: 1px solid black; border-bottom: 1px solid black; /* The directional border-bottom */ } ``` In versions 5.3.0 and 5.3.1, the above code may cause the "border-bottom" to stop rendering properly, and it might appear as if there is no "border-bottom" for the cells. To fix this issue, you can use the "border-collapse" property on the table element, which can resolve the problem with directional borders: CSS (with fix): ``` .my-table { border-collapse: collapse; /* Add this line to fix the issue */ } .my-table td { border: 1px solid black; /* Combine all borders into one shorthand property */ } ``` By setting the "border-collapse" property to "collapse," the directional "border-bottom" should work as expected, and you will see all borders rendered correctly for the table cells.
