author: dailytabs

body:
I’m not surprised by the current Bootstrap rendering, but I don’t have a particularly strong opinion on it either.

Bootstrap already provides a solid set of utilities and customization options that make it easy to override default styles. Developers can apply custom classes, use utility classes for things like spacing or text styling, and even redefine Sass variables to ensure consistent, project-specific designs.

That said, in some tools or plugins, Bootstrap’s CSS may be applied automatically to Markdown content without an easy way to override the rendering. In those cases, it’s important that headers have some visual distinction from normal text. Font size is one option, but not the only one—other styling cues like boldness, color, or spacing can also help. Interestingly, if I remember correctly, in tools like Microsoft Word, the equivalent of `<h6>` is sometimes smaller than body text, but is still visually distinct thanks to its different texture or style.

**However, IMHO, if any changes were to be made to the default header styling, they should be introduced in a major version update.** Headers and their related utilities are deeply integrated into many existing projects, and modifying them could cause widespread issues. Even with Bootstrap’s flexibility, changing default behavior can lead to unexpected side effects or require significant effort to adapt.

For context, here’s how Markdown rendered with Primer styles looks:

---

```
# h1
Normal text

## h2
Normal text

### h3
Normal text

#### h4
Normal text

##### h5
Normal text

###### h6
Normal text
```

# h1
Normal text

## h2
Normal text

### h3
Normal text

#### h4
Normal text

##### h5
Normal text

###### h6
Normal text

author: SPBCodes

body:
Bootstrap's use of `var(--bs-secondary-color)` for `.form-text` is intentional to create visual hierarchy: the `.form-text` is meant to appear more subdued than labels or main content (i.e., a "secondary" text color). So changing it to `inherit` universally would undermine that design choice.

With the current CSS architecture, this isn't something we could—or would necessarily want to—change easily.

The approach suggested by @viniciusvts using `.text-reset` works if you want `.form-text` to match the surrounding text/label color (e.g., white in a dark background).

Another approach would be to add `data-bs-theme="dark"` to the container (or a sub-container) to benefit from Bootstrap’s dark mode theming contextually, including for `.form-text`.

Closing as nothing to do on Bootstrap's side but you can continue the discussion if needed :)

author: nathan-muir

body:
Thanks for reporting this issue @dibsyjr1 
I can reproduce directly in the documentation. I can confirm that the issue comes from 24305e7b187875c524a3c9c97ae53e47c1cc5b03.

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/3854ece2-d206-407a-9e79-97095ed1f010)

There's another issue with tab navigation from one dropdown to another:

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/f5792477-d3b1-4bcb-b065-a989d75cec2e)

---

I did a quick local test with the following approach. It seemed to improve things, but also felt overly complex. There’s likely a cleaner or more efficient solution.

```diff
diff --git a/js/src/dropdown.js b/js/src/dropdown.js
index 88af932f7..7e608998e 100644
--- a/js/src/dropdown.js
+++ b/js/src/dropdown.js
@@ -208,8 +208,17 @@ class Dropdown extends BaseComponent {
     Manipulator.removeDataAttribute(this._menu, 'popper')
     EventHandler.trigger(this._element, EVENT_HIDDEN, relatedTarget)
 
-    // Explicitly return focus to the trigger element
-    this._element.focus()
+    // Return focus to trigger element if:
+    // 1. The dropdown was closed via keyboard (Escape key)
+    // 2. The dropdown was closed by clicking a dropdown item
+    // 3. The dropdown was closed by clicking outside (but not on a focusable element)
+    // 4. The dropdown was closed by tabbing to another dropdown (let the browser handle focus)
+    const isTabNavigation = relatedTarget.clickEvent?.type === 'keyup' && relatedTarget.clickEvent?.key === TAB_KEY
+    if (!isTabNavigation && (!relatedTarget.clickEvent ||
+        (relatedTarget.clickEvent && this._menu.contains(relatedTarget.clickEvent.target)) ||
+        (relatedTarget.clickEvent && !SelectorEngine.focusableChildren(document.body).includes(relatedTarget.clickEvent.target)))) {
+      this._element.focus()
+    }
   }
 
   _getConfig(config) {
@@ -379,14 +388,14 @@ class Dropdown extends BaseComponent {
         continue
       }
 
-      // Tab navigation through the dropdown menu or events from contained inputs shouldn't close the menu
+      // Don't close the dropdown if clicking on a focusable element inside the menu
       if (context._menu.contains(event.target) && ((event.type === 'keyup' && event.key === TAB_KEY) || /input|select|option|textarea|form/i.test(event.target.tagName))) {
         continue
       }
 
       const relatedTarget = { relatedTarget: context._element }
 
-      if (event.type === 'click') {
+      if (event.type === 'click' || event.type === 'keyup') {
         relatedTarget.clickEvent = event
       }
```
author: julien-deramond

body:
I also stumbled across this issue, see comment in #35793 and before reading this issue I was guessing that just preventing the focus on parent if you click inside the dropdown would be enough.

Now I see that there is maybe more about this ...

Some general thoughts:
#35793 was about A11y and "returning focus to parent element if dropdown is closed".

I suppose this is in someways about the definition of "closing". 
By default (from the autoClose option default setting): 

> "the dropdown will be closed by clicking outside or inside the dropdown menu"

The dropdown will be closed on any click basically. If this is the definition for "closing", returning the focus to the parent element is correct in terms of the A11y requirement "returning focus to parent if dropdown is closed".

For the example given on clicking an input outside the dropdown: In theory it might also be an "accident" clicking an input element. Just clicking outside the dropdown (maybe to close it) and hitting an input element (in the example given with the bootstrap documentation, this seems rather silly. But I can imagine a dropdown that covers lot of the screen and maybe there is just part of an input showing behind the dropdown. In such a case it might not be that unlikely to accidentally hit the input trying to close the dropdown by clicking outside of it).

So while I see the points made here, I think in someways there is a mixup between two actions (close and click). And it cannot be determined reliably in any case if the click means just closing the dropdown (and returning focus to parent) or if it means closing the dropdown and doing some other action (that implies not returning the focus to parent). That said, I suppose that "close" means "close" and thus means returning focus to parent. One can use the autoClose option to further control "closing".

body:
In Bootstrap's floating label implementation (`.form-floating`), the placeholder must be present but is not shown visibly. It serves only a technical purpose — it triggers the input to show the floating label correctly when the input is empty.

So, you must include a non-empty placeholder attribute, but the placeholder will not display as a visible hint like in regular inputs, only the label will be displayed.

Edit: created https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/pull/41526 to try to improve the explanation in our docs.

author: julien-deramond

body:
a lot of tall claims and fundamental misunderstandings about WCAG here ...

let's start with: WCAG does not care about tooltips not displaying on hover. your content couldn't rely on those anyway, as otherwise you'd be breaking 2.1.1 Keyboard, since native tooltips don't show for keyboard users even when something IS focusable. and for controls that are not keyboard focusable, even custom tooltips won't show as you can't set focus to the control.

next up, labels or instructions ... only applies to cases where the user needs to enter/choose information (text inputs, selects, radio buttons, checkboxes, etc). not individual buttons or links.

what i'd say, more fundamentally: you seem to have a situation where you disabled controls, and the only way for a user to know why it's disabled is because you added some tooltip for it. that's bad UX. the fact that bootstrap doesn't let you do this is not a failure of bootstrap, but of the UX you chose. and your claims of WCAG failure are ... misinformed at best.
author: patrickhlauke

body:
> `<button disabled title="...">` will show a tooltip on hover

congrats, this *will* fail 2.1.1 Keyboard ...
author: patrickhlauke

body:
Understood, thanks for the clarification.

Just to add — for a disabled button with a tooltip, even when I follow the suggested workaround of wrapping it in a <span> with the tooltip, VoiceOver does not read out the tooltip when navigating through the disabled button using keyboard.

I've attached a video demonstrating this. 

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/aecef10d-bf9e-43b3-8620-43bff1fafc04

Don’t you see this as an accessibility issue for users with blindness who rely on screen readers like VoiceOver?

cc: @patrickhlauke
author: ashik-ts-12418

body:
@ashik-ts-12418 To avoid the issue, I would suggest to instead use `aria-disabled`.

e.g.

```html
<button type="button" class="btn btn-primary disabled" aria-disabled="true">
  Primary button
</button>
```

That way it'll be announced as disabled and focusable. But I wish that `aria-disabled` buttons had the same CSS applied as disabled buttons automatically (without needing `disabled` class) and I made a GitHub Issue to request it.
https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/issues/39727
author: coliff

body:
@coliff Thanks for the suggestion!

Using `aria-disabled` does help make the button focusable and announces it as “dimmed” or “disabled” in VoiceOver — which is good. But the core issue still remains:

> **VoiceOver does not announce the tooltip even when using `aria-disabled` and wrapping it in a `<span>`.**

So while `aria-disabled` solves the focusability and state announcement part, it doesn’t fix the fact that **users with blindness aren’t informed why the button is disabled** — which was the original goal of having a tooltip in the first place.

That’s why I believe this is still an accessibility concern that goes beyond just whether the button is reachable/focusable.
author: ashik-ts-12418

author: julien-deramond

body:
Thanks a lot for reporting this use case with a CodePen, @steffen-harbich-cognitum 🙏  
I can confirm the rendering issue on macOS with Chrome, Edge, and Safari, while it works correctly in Firefox.  
Looks like this behavior existed even before https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/commit/c5bec4ea7bd74b679fc2ecc53c141bff3750915b and #37533.

In this specific case, adding `.position-fixed` to the `.visually-hidden` `<span>` appears to fix the issue by fully removing it from the layout flow, which prevents Chrome (and others) from collapsing the column width. However, this may cause the hidden content to become visually or semantically detached from the table context, so use with care.

It seems to be a rendering quirk in Chromium and WebKit-based browsers related to how table layout is calculated when a column contains only visually hidden content. Firefox handles this differently by preserving column width.

For now, I’d say the fix likely needs to come from the outside:
- either by cautiously applying `.position-fixed` (while ensuring a11y is preserved)
- or by exploring alternatives like setting a `min-width`, inserting a layout-visible spacer (e.g. a ` ` outside `.visually-hidden`), or using `table-layout: fixed`, depending on the use case 🤷

If anyone has ideas or references in browsers trackers 🙏 

Please also note that the issue is not observable in https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/pull/41474 as it doesn't use the `position: absolute`, but a `display: inline-block`.

Maybe an interesting use case for you @ffoodd who maintains this French article https://www.ffoodd.fr/masquage-accessible-de-pointe/
author: julien-deramond

body:
Hi! I'm new to GitHub contributions and just wanted to confirm that the ` ` fix mentioned above works for me too.

I tested it in Chrome by adding ` ` after the `.visually-hidden` span in the table header, and the body scrollbar issue was resolved without breaking accessibility.

Thanks for the detailed explanation and example! This was really helpful. 😊
author: prachimaskar

body: Thanks for looking into it. When I add the ` ` in front or after the `<span class="visually-hidden">` in my CodePen example, it still shows the scrollbar in Chrome for me. However, the approach with "display: inline-block" works well. @julien-deramond, do you see any negative impact of this approach in the table? As far as I tested, everything was as expected.
author: steffen-harbich-cognitum

body:
> do you see any negative impact of this approach in the table?

I don’t see any immediate issues with this approach, but it will likely depend on your actual use case beyond the CodePen example. In your real project, I’d pay particular attention to things like table width, responsiveness, keyboard focus (especially if the `<td>`, whose header uses a `.visually-hidden` class, contains focusable elements), and screen reader behavior.

If everything renders correctly, keyboard navigation works as expected, and screen readers interpret the structure properly, then this is probably a solid solution for your specific scenario.
author: julien-deramond

body:
Following the discussion above, I tested the `display: inline-block` approach applied to the `.visually-hidden` span inside the table header.

🔍 **Test Environment:**  
Chrome (latest), standard desktop viewport, using `.my-table-wrapper { overflow-x: auto; }` and `td { white-space: nowrap; }`.

✅ **Results:**  
- No horizontal scrollbar appeared  
- Table layout remained stable across zoom levels and window resizing  
- Focusable elements (`<button>`) inside `<td>` worked correctly with keyboard navigation  
- `.visually-hidden` content remained in the DOM and semantically valid for screen readers (though I couldn't fully verify audio output)

📌 **Conclusion:**  
This fix appears stable and does not negatively impact layout, accessibility, or interaction in common use cases. Like Julien mentioned, edge cases (e.g. RTL layouts or complex headers with focusable elements) may still need individual testing.

Thanks to everyone involved — this testing helped me understand the importance of accessibility-aware styling in real-world components.

author: mdo

body:
Thanks for the suggestion @Nicoloye!

While Bootstrap doesn’t provide per-component CSS files in the `dist/` folder, you can already achieve this level of control using the Sass source files. [By importing only the components you need into your custom Sass entry file](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/customize/optimize/#lean-sass-imports), you can compile a slimmed-down CSS build. You can also combine this with tools like [PurgeCSS](https://purgecss.com/) to remove unused styles at build time for even better optimization.

On the JS side, Bootstrap 5+ supports ES module imports for each component, so [you can include only the JavaScript you actually use](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/getting-started/javascript/). This modular approach gives you full control over what gets bundled, making it easier to meet performance or eco-design goals without having to recompile the entire framework.

Just out of curiosity, since it's related to this feature request, have you considered using [Sass optimizations](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/customize/optimize/) to specify which parts of Bootstrap are being included?
Additionally, tools like [PurgeCSS](https://purgecss.com/) can help remove unused CSS, which we generally recommend as a best practice.
Of course, we understand this might not be feasible in every scenario, but it's usually a helpful approach.

author: ThomasLandauer

body:
Hey Thomas 👋  I really like your idea, and I totally get the need for this kind of tool when you're trying to keep things lean for projects like dompdf.

Just wanted to mention, while it would be amazing to have an official Bootstrap tool, you don’t have to wait for the open source team to build it (they already have a lot on their plate). you can actually use ChatGPT to accomplish this right now. There’s a feature called Projects where you can upload files like bootstrap.css, and GPT will go through them with you. You can then ask it to extract specific class definitions, resolve var(--...) values, and even generate a clean output for just the components you need, or just paste the class name like .card, and ask it to resolve all the CSS variables to actual values based on the latest Bootstrap release. It’s surprisingly accurate and super handy for quick copy-paste use. Its useful for exactly the kind of work you're doing.

Might save you a lot of time, and no need to wait for an official tool. 😉

author: mdo

body: The issue arises due to a compatibility problem between Bootstrap's form-floating labels and the validation mechanism when used within input-group elements. Specifically, when validation is applied to input-group-text elements combined with form-floating divs, the layout expands incorrectly. This happens because the form-floating class relies on specific CSS positioning that conflicts with the default validation styling in Bootstrap 5.3.7. The proposed fix of replacing the div with a fieldset aims to address this, but it is poorly documented. Additionally, placing validation divs outside the form structure disrupts the grouping, causing the layout to break. This behavior is observed in the provided HTML structure on Windows with Firefox.

https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/blob/3b9a4167acf2a5d4e4b95fcafe2dd0459ef637a8/js/src/carousel.js#L169-L188

`to(index)` does not validate for type but then immediately does:

Indicating that a `Number` is expected.

Your docs for v5 still indicate that a string should be passed.

```
const myCarouselEl = document.querySelector('#myCarousel')
const carousel = bootstrap.Carousel.getInstance(myCarouselEl) // Retrieve a Carousel instance

myCarouselEl.addEventListener('slid.bs.carousel', event => {
  carousel.to('2') // Will slide to the slide 2 as soon as the transition to slide 1 is finished
})

carousel.to('1') // Will start sliding to the slide 1 and returns to the caller
carousel.to('2') // !! Will be ignored, as the transition to the slide 1 is not finished !!
```

https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/getting-started/javascript/#asynchronous-functions-and-transitions


While you're fixing your v5 docs perhaps you could take a moment to fix the same errors in your currently published and very much still live v4 docs.

author: mdo

body:
The use of custom properties in the current version of Bootstrap is somewhat inconsistent. After reviewing the history of `--bs-form-check-bg`, it seems it was introduced unintentionally. Originally, it was defined as:

```scss
--#{$prefix}form-check-bg: #{escape-svg($form-check-input-bg)};
```

This was meant to enable light/dark mode handling for an image, but since it was actually a background color, it was later changed to:

```scss
--#{$prefix}form-check-bg: #{$form-check-input-bg};
```

For that reason, I don’t believe this custom property was deliberately planned in the first place. I’m keeping the issue open, as our guidelines for what should or shouldn’t be a custom property are still unclear.

Let’s maybe revisit this use case in an upcoming minor or major release.
author: julien-deramond

body:
@julien-deramond 

Thank you for the quick response and for the clarification. It is very helpful to know that the **--bs-form-check-bg** variable was originally introduced unintentionally.

Your point about the guidelines for custom properties being unclear got me thinking. I believe this dilemma can be resolved by focusing on the primary purpose of these variables: to enable the dynamic adjustment of styles at runtime.

With that in mind, a simple rule emerges:

**Any CSS property whose value is likely to be adjusted at runtime should be exposed as a custom property (CSS variable).**

The background-color of a :checked element is a perfect example. The colors of active elements are typically part of a theme, which is designed to be changed dynamically based on the application's state. When the unchecked state uses a variable, but the checked state does not, it leads to the exact inconsistency we identified in our project.

I hope this perspective helps clarify the guidelines and contributes to a more consistent and flexible solution for dynamic theming in future versions.

pc
author: true-perfect-code

body:
> I believe this dilemma can be resolved by focusing on the primary purpose of these variables: to enable the dynamic adjustment of styles at runtime.

Right now, we’re in an in-between state: we introduced custom properties mainly for dark mode, not for all colors to be customized "from outside". Until now, most customization was handled via Sass. Now it’s a mix of both approaches, and inconsistent. There’s still a heavily drafted v6, so let’s see how we can improve it :)
author: julien-deramond

body:
@julien-deramond 

Thanks for the thoughtful and honest response. I think "in-between state" perfectly captures the current situation.

Your explanation that custom properties were primarily introduced for dark mode is very insightful. It helps to understand the root cause of this inconsistency. This perspective is what I'd like to build on.

**What about a New Perspective: Accessibility as the Guiding Principle?**

Perhaps the "dilemma" over when to use custom properties can be resolved by considering a different, more pressing need: accessibility (A11y). The requirements for accessible applications have grown significantly, both legally (e.g., stricter EU regulations) and demographically (e.g., an aging population with diverse needs).

A one-size-fits-all design, no matter how well-crafted, will not be accessible for all users. A developer's ability to easily adjust an app's theme at runtime is no longer a fringe feature—it's a necessity.

This is where the dynamic nature of CSS variables becomes essential. They are the perfect tool to allow developers to:
- Provide high-contrast themes for users with visual impairments.
- Adjust font and element (button, checkbox...) sizes and colors on the fly.
- Meet specific user needs for a personalized and accessible experience.

In this light, the question is not whether to introduce custom properties, but rather, where they are necessary to build a future-proof and inclusive framework. It seems clear that any element property that is a part of a theme, like the background-color of a checked checkbox, should be exposed as a custom property. This would empower developers to build accessible applications without having to resort to complex workarounds.

Moving forward, fully embracing custom properties for all theming-related aspects seems less like an uncertain path and more like a clear, necessary strategy for Bootstrap to remain a relevant and essential framework.

pc
author: true-perfect-code

body:
**...and perhaps another, potentially decisive reason for the complete adoption of CSS variables:**

The biggest criticism of Bootstrap has always been the "Bootstrap look" and the perceived lack of flexibility. With a full conversion to custom properties, this criticism could be eliminated entirely. A developer could use Bootstrap's components for their speed and structure, but then **apply a completely unique design system simply by overriding a few key variables**, both at the global (:root) and component-specific levels.

This would give Bootstrap a significant competitive advantage. It would combine the best of both worlds: the speed of a component-based framework with the flexibility of a utility-first approach like Tailwind CSS. It's a powerful argument that would **not only improve the framework's accessibility but also appeal directly to the developers who currently prefer Tailwind**.

This seems like the most strategic path forward for Bootstrap to solidify its position in the modern web development landscape.

pc
author: true-perfect-code

body: Closing as this has been revisited in v6-dev.
author: mdo

body: Thank you for the feedback. One of the main reasons many developers still avoid Bootstrap is its "classic Bootstrap look" that often feels dated. However, as shown in our P11 library, the extensive use of CSS custom properties (--bs-*) makes it extremely easy to completely transform the default Bootstrap appearance into any custom design — with just a button click and at runtime: -> https://pa11y.true-perfect-code.ch/themestyle --- **Classic bootstrap:** --- **Example 1 Customized using --bs- :** --- **Example 2 Customized using --bs- :** --- **Or create look like other Blazor UI Lib:** --- This gives developers enormous flexibility for White-Label solutions, corporate designs, and modern native-ish UIs without recompiling anything. It could attract many new users who currently dismiss Bootstrap because of its visual style. Our open-source P11 Blazor UI library (comming soon) (for Web, MAUI Blazor Hybrid, WPF, and Capacitor) relies heavily on consistent --bs-* variables for: - Full runtime theming (Light/Dark/Custom brands) via IThemeService - Scoped overrides and dynamic color mapping - Seamless switching between designs without reload See also: https://pa11y.true-perfect-code.ch/variablescolor **Please keep the CSS variables approach consistent and powerful in Bootstrap 6. It’s one of Bootstrap’s greatest modern strengths and would be a big loss if weakened.** Thank you for your great work! pc team

author: bigdogwillfeed

body:
@bigdogwillfeed I haven't debugged it, but I have a feeling that this is a regression introduced by https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/pull/41365.

I think it might be some kind of race condition. The element within the dropdown opens the modal. After the modal opened the dropdown closes, causing the focus to be explicitly set to the dropdown. This causes the focus trap of the modal to kick in and pulling it back into the modal, selecting the first element - the close button in that case.

There might be other side effects as such mdo may have decided to revert that change here https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/pull/41668.

**TLDR;** with the latest version (5.3.8 [here](https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/bootstrap@5.3.8/dist/js/bootstrap.bundle.min.js)) this should be solved. If you would like, you can test it with that version and see if it still happens.

body:
This seems to be a usage issue rather than a bug. The `.popover-header` class isn’t intended to be used on its own, it should always be nested inside an element with the `.popover` class. The two aren’t independent; `.popover-header` styles only apply within a proper popover structure. That’s why the CSS variables aren’t taking effect in your CodePen example.

I’ll close this issue for now, but feel free to provide more details if I’ve misunderstood the context 🙂
author: julien-deramond

body: @julien-deramond Thank you for looking into it! I agree it was probably never proper use to begin with. It is probably less necessary because the CSS variables cause the dependence on the parent now for actual values, but I'm wondering if it would better reveal the intention if the definition for `.popover-header` was nested inside of `.popover` to more clearly prevent independent use which was possible in previous versions.

author: mdo

body:
Hey!

The issue you're experiencing is actually expected behavior when placing a modal inside an offcanvas.

The official Bootstrap recommendation:

> Whenever possible, place your modal HTML in a top-level position to avoid potential interference from other elements. You’ll likely run into issues when nesting a `.modal` within another fixed element.

Source: [How it works](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.0/components/modal/#how-it-works)

Why this happens:

- Offcanvas creates its backdrop with `rootElement: this._element.parentNode`.
- Modal creates its backdrop with default `rootElement: 'body'`.
- When modal is nested inside offcanvas, the modal element stays inside offcanvas (with its z-index context), but the modal backdrop gets appended to body — causing z-index conflicts.

Workaround: disable modal's backdrop since offcanvas already has one.

body:
Hi @lalithaar,

The `.btn-outline-light` and `.btn-outline-dark` classes are button variants that *don’t adapt* to light or dark mode. In other words, `.btn-outline-light` always stays light, so it should only be used on dark surfaces, even in light mode. Conversely, `.btn-outline-dark` stays dark and should only be used on light surfaces.

In your example, `.btn-outline-light` isn’t appropriate because it’s placed on a light background.

We realize this behavior isn’t very intuitive, it's inherited from when light and dark modes were not available, even though the documentation mentions:

> Some of the button styles use a relatively light foreground color, and should only be used on a dark background in order to have sufficient contrast.

This will likely be improved in v6, so I'm closing this one.

body:
Proposal: Native CSS & JS Purge in Bootstrap

Bootstrap is a very solid framework, but CSS and JS bundles are still heavy in production, and proper optimization often relies on external tools (PurgeCSS, PostCSS configs, etc.), which increases complexity—especially for modern projects.

Proposal: add an official, optional native tool to Bootstrap that:

automatically analyzes project files (.html, .js, .jsx, .vue, etc.)

detects actually used Bootstrap classes

generates a minimal CSS build

includes only the required JS components (Modal, Dropdown, etc.)

All of this with zero configuration by default, plus an optional safelist for dynamic classes.

Benefits:

significantly smaller CSS & JS bundles

better performance (especially on mobile)

simpler developer experience

keeps Bootstrap competitive with modern solutions

Thanks for the amazing work on Bootstrap

body: v6-dev rebuilds the accordion on native `<details name>` elements with no JavaScript, so the browser enforces single-item exclusivity synchronously — two items can't be open at once. Verified you can't double-open even by toggling all items (or forcing `.open = true` on all of them) in the same tick; the old JS `_isTransitioning` race no longer applies. Closing as resolved in v6-dev — please open a new issue if you can reproduce it on a native-`<details>` accordion there.

body:
More observations:

* If the modal is the last element on the page, then using Tab will move focus past the modal to the browser's UI
* If the modal is the first element on the page, then using Shift-Tab will move focus outside the modal to the browser's top UI
* In either case, there is a brief moment that focus can go outside the modal to another element in the DOM, which the browser will automatically scroll into view before trapping the focus back to the modal.  I am not sure how to override this seemingly default browser behavior.

body:
Thanks for reporting this, @AlbertWiersch, great catch!

I’m not deeply familiar with these WebKit datetime selectors, and this one was introduced about five years ago in Bootstrap. From a quick scan of docs and usage examples online (CodePen, etc.), I couldn’t find any historical references to `::-webkit-datetime-edit-minute`. The only occurrences I found were in Bootstrap-related code.

`::-webkit-datetime-edit-minute-field` does indeed seem to be the correct selector to use here.

We’d need to verify compatibility with our Browserslistrc configuration and confirm that it behaves as expected, but this could be a valid fix, either for v5 or to be integrated directly into v6.

/cc @mdo
author: julien-deramond

body:
That's a great catch!

This is probably related to #42007.

Inspecting the css in reboot:
```
::-webkit-datetime-edit-fields-wrapper,
::-webkit-datetime-edit-text,
::-webkit-datetime-edit-minute,
::-webkit-datetime-edit-hour-field,
::-webkit-datetime-edit-day-field,
::-webkit-datetime-edit-month-field,
::-webkit-datetime-edit-year-field {
  padding: 0;
}
```

All, but the minute filed get padding: 0. That's why it may be misaligned.

body:
Bootstrap no longer depends on jQuery, as the dependency was fully removed starting with v5.

The warning you referenced comes from Bootstrap 4, not Bootstrap 5:
https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/blob/v4.6.0/js/src/util.js#L189

Bootstrap 5 itself does not require or check for any jQuery version. See [this](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/getting-started/javascript/#optionally-using-jquery)
author: marcsoler

body: Update: this change reintroduces a plethora of older issues (such as this one: https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/issues/35553), and creates new ones too. Since the `$font-size-base` is now `14px`, the `-sm` classes/calculations, their computed values will now be at `12.25px` because the ratio of -sm is .875, so pixel divisions start happening. This can be easily observed on normal DPI screens, see: Furthermore this also affects `-xs` classes/calculations too, since their computed values will now be at `10.5px`, because the ratio of `-xs` is .75. In this current form, https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/issues/35553 will happen by default on Bootstrap on pretty much every component. My proposal is to switch back to `$font-size-base` set to `1rem` so that `sm` becomes `14px`, and `xs` becomes `12px`, so no pixel division happens. And if the preferred way is to have smaller texts in dropdowns and other elements, we can simply apply the `$font-size-sm` to them instead of the `$font-size-base`, that way you can keep the text smallers, without having any clamping issues. Alternatively, if you really want to stick with `$font-size-base` set to `14px`, to avoid line clamping, will need to choose different ratios for `sm` and `xs` so that no pixel divisions happen.

author: mdo

body:
I believe you should take into consideration the context of those apps.

Design trends come and go, in 2-4 years we might be defaulting back to 16px.

Most of the apps that are practicing 14px (and even frameworks, such as shadcn ui), have a lot of components ready made for large/complex dashboards, such as the likes of: `ui.shadcn.com`, `catalyst.tailwind.com`, etc, so they kinda have to cather to that, this is especially true since they don't provide "-lg", "-base", and "-small" variants.

It's much easier to just use "small" class to bring everything down to 14px, than vice-versa, due to how padding works (this is what catalyst is doing).

By designing the paddings to work with 16px, when applying `small` on an element, the padding won't feel too small.

By contrast, if paddings will be calculated for `14px` elements, applying the base font size utility won't be sufficient, and additional padding will need to be added.

Yes, we'll have the option to tinker with paddings, as they're variables, but you can reduce the overhead for when this will go live.

Another thing that I've noticed is with inputs.

BS5 practiced `38px` input height, with a `16px` font size, with the same height & size for buttons.
BS6 went to `40px` input height, and `14px` in font size (smaller font to read, larger paddings), while buttons have shrinked in height considerably. I don't see how inline forms will work with the current input height, the paddings for buttons will be exaggerated at 40px.

I'd be willing to contribute and/or provide feedback if there's a thread where you're discussing certain design decisions. I understand that this is still in very early stages, and you're still experimenting/testing, which is the right thing to do.

P.S. I love the direction BS6 is going with the new OTP inputs, chip inputs, inverted, date range pickers, button variants, etc. The world really needs a framework that doesn't require half of the npm repository (and react) just to have a functional webpage 😅.

body:
I really really appreciate the thoughtful and critical response here. Please don’t hesitate to keep it coming. The delivery and approach is well received :).

Regarding ongoing feedback and general questions/ideas, I’ve opened https://github.com/orgs/twbs/discussions/42090 to consolidate v6-dev feedback. You can continue to post things there or even in issues/PRs if you like.

I’ll swing back and see about updating the base font-size and those input sizes. I do agree some things have felt big while others have felt small. In general I’m trying to figure out what the new visual language looks like for Bootstrap in 2026. I really feel like leaning into more opinionated styles with easy overrides is the path forward.

I’m also in a slight holding pattern on Sass stuff and would love feedback on an idea I had to consolidate our approach to Sass/CSS customization. See https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/pull/41983. That’ll really affect how much components continue to change internally.

Let me know what you think of all that and any other ideas/comments.

author: martinszeltins

body:
@patrickhlauke also, aren't you disagreeing with standards?

Microsoft’s design guidelines:
> It is imperative not to use the hand pointer for other purposes. For example, command buttons already have a strong affordance, so they don’t need a hand pointer. The hand pointer must mean “this target is a link” and nothing else.

Apple’s Human Interface Guidelines:
> The content beneath the pointer is a URL link to a webpage, document, or other item.

W3C User Interface Guidelines:
> The cursor is a pointer that indicates a link.

So it seems to me like this is your own personal preference which goes against established standards.
author: martinszeltins

body:
Just to add my two cents to this discussion.

I may not be the authority on accessibility and user experience like @patrickhlauke :heart:, but I’ve been a frontend developer for a long time and have had this conversation before.  And I have a clear opinion on this matter:

I always use the pointer cursor on interactive elements, regardless of whether they’re links or buttons (which can serve the same purpose in some layouts).

There was a discussion on the spec repository a while ago that resolved the issue by stating that authors should use the pointer cursor on links and may use it on other interactive elements. (https://github.com/w3c/csswg-drafts/issues/1936#issuecomment-420722704)

To further support my reluctance to adopt this change, there’s another currently open bug issue on the Shadcn repository with 114 upvotes and only 1 downvote. (https://github.com/shadcn-ui/ui/issues/6843#issue-2892042768)

The mentioned change in Tailwind CSS was introduced by https://github.com/tailwindlabs/tailwindcss/issues/8961, which has 41 downvotes compared to 15 upvotes.

From the top 10 most visited websites according to Wikipedia:
1. Google - uses the pointer cursor on their buttons.
2. YouTube - uses the pointer cursor on their buttons.
3. Facebook - uses the pointer cursor on their buttons.
4. Instagram - uses the pointer cursor on their buttons.
5. ChatGPT - uses the pointer cursor on their buttons.
6. X - uses the pointer cursor on their buttons.
7. Reddit - uses the pointer cursor on their buttons.
8. Wikipedia - uses the pointer cursor on their buttons.
9. WhatsApp - uses the pointer cursor on their buttons.
10. Bing - uses the pointer cursor on their buttons. 
(On a side note, GitHub also uses the pointer cursor on their buttons.)

I believe it’s common enough to have the pointer cursor on interactive elements that we should keep it the way it is.

If someone wants to opt out of this behavior, there’s a variable `$enable-button-pointers` that can be used to disable it.

Regards.

For example, how would I know that the "accessibility", "css" and "feature" labels on this page were interactable from looking at them alone?

author: Tripp-Sautner

body: I also don't think that Bootstrap should remove the hand cursor on buttons by default. While it's true that operating systems don't recommend using the hand cursor for buttons, and in an ideal world we shouldn't have to use it, we must make a distinction here, which is that the web is vastly different that an operating system. I have a very good example on why it would be a bad idea to remove the hand cursor. For example, `btn-subtle theme-inverse` naturally has a higher color contrast than `btn-subtle theme-secondary`, but when `btn-inverse` is in a **disabled** state, it gets faded, therefore it makes it closely resemble a `btn-subtle theme-secondary` **active** state. You can see how hard it is to visually distinguish between these two. Now, without the visual cue of the cursor hand, how would one know which is active & clickable, and which isn't? Operating systems don't have to deal with such cases, because they have a very well defined number of button variants. Bootstrap supports theming, and it's bound to have such cases. For example, one could use a higher green contrast for the `primary` theme, so when a primary theme button is **disabled**, it would make it resemble a `btn-subtle theme-success` in an **active** state. Since Bootstrap already has a SaaS variable that allows disabling this hand cursor, I don't think this should be considered an issue. Perhaps moving the cursor attribute style from a SaaS variable to a CSS variable instead would be a better idea instead? That way one could make his choice without recompiling the CSS.

author: mdo

body:
Okay yeah,this is a bug no soubt

On Tue, Mar 17, 2026, 7:44 AM Denis Washington ***@***.***>
wrote:

> *denisw* created an issue (twbs/bootstrap#42192)
> <https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/issues/42192>
> Prerequisites
>
>    - I have searched
>    <https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/issues?utf8=%E2%9C%93&q=is%3Aissue>
>    for duplicate or closed issues
>    - I have validated <https://html5.validator.nu/> any HTML to avoid
>    common problems
>    - I have read the contributing guidelines
>    <https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/blob/main/.github/CONTRIBUTING.md>
>
> Describe the issue
>
> In our app, we want the container max width to not grow from the md
> breakpoint onwards. However, this triggers the following warning from
> Bootstrap's -assert-ascending():
>
> WARNING: Invalid value for $container-max-widths: This map must be in ascending order, but key 'xxl' has value 38.5rem which isn't greater than 38.5rem, the value of the previous key 'xl' !
>
> As there is nothing wrong with not growing the max-width at some point, I
> don't think this should be warning. Checking that the values are

>   md: 616px,
>   lg: 616px,
>   xl: 616px,
>   xxl: 616px,
> );
> @import "bootstrap";
>

> Firefox

author: connorbell1231ho-source

body: Interesting approach. Would you not want to simply remove the larger values from the map instead? Why keep those around?
author: mdo

body:
> Interesting approach. Would you not want to simply remove the larger values from the map instead? Why keep those around?

To be honest, I just ‘t think of doing that! I thought that I had to provide a value for every breakpoint. If specifying only `sm` and `md` works (will confirm tomorrow), then this issue can be closed, of course.
author: denisw

body: Ya removing or not specifying the higher breakpoint values (depending on how you're overriding) should work for you because each breakpoint is always higher than the last. Our media queries are min-width based, and the min-width value for `md` will always be reached at the higher tiers.
author: mdo

body: Feel free to open a new issue or comment here if needed.

author: jeessmokat-hue

body:
Thank you for bringing up this issue. I can see that the ascending check in
Bootstrap's $container-max-widths is causing a warning when attempting to
set equal values for the max-widths at certain breakpoints, specifically at
the md breakpoint and beyond.

I agree that, while the values should be monotonically increasing, having
equal values (without growth) shouldn’t trigger a warning. This is
particularly important in cases where developers intend to have no growth
at a particular breakpoint.

It would be helpful to consider updating the logic to allow for equal
values without showing a warning, as long as the values are stable. This
could prevent unnecessary issues for developers trying to fine-tune their
layouts without violating the intended container behavior.

Please let me know if you need further details or would like assistance in
repro

On Thu, Mar 19, 2026, 2:06 PM simmonsharper247-oss ***@***.***>
wrote:

> *simmonsharper247-oss* left a comment (twbs/bootstrap#42192)
> <https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/issues/42192#issuecomment-4090392939>
> Thanks for your advice.
>
> On Thu, Mar 19, 2026, 7:45 AM nicovale1231-maker ***@***.***>
> wrote:
>
> > *nicovale1231-maker* left a comment (twbs/bootstrap#42192)
> > <https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/issues/42192#issuecomment-4089882427>
>
> > Thank you for bringing up this issue. I can see that the ascending check
> > in
> > Bootstrap's $container-max-widths is causing a warning when attempting
> to
> > set equal values for the max-widths at certain breakpoints, specifically
> > at
> > the md breakpoint and beyond.
> >
> > I agree that, while the values should be monotonically increasing,
> having
> > equal values (without growth) shouldn’t trigger a warning. This is
> > particularly important in cases where developers intend to have no
> growth
> > at a particular breakpoint.
> >
> > It would be helpful to consider updating the logic to allow for equal
> > values without showing a warning, as long as the values are stable. This
> > could prevent unnecessary issues for developers trying to fine-tune
> their
> > layouts without violating the intended container behavior.
> >
> > Please let me know if you need further details or would like assistance
> in
> > repro
> >
> > On Tue, Mar 17, 2026, 7:44 AM Denis Washington ***@***.***>
> > wrote:
> >
> > > *denisw* created an issue (twbs/bootstrap#42192)
> > > <https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/issues/42192>
> > > Prerequisites
> > >
> > > - I have searched
> > > <https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/issues?utf8=%E2%9C%93&q=is%3Aissue>
>
> > > for duplicate or closed issues
> > > - I have validated <https://html5.validator.nu/> any HTML to avoid
> > > common problems
> > > - I have read the contributing guidelines
> > > <https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/blob/main/.github/CONTRIBUTING.md>
> > >
> > > Describe the issue
> > >
> > > In our app, we want the container max width to not grow from the md
> > > breakpoint onwards. However, this triggers the following warning from
> > > Bootstrap's -assert-ascending():
> > >
> > > WARNING: Invalid value for $container-max-widths: This map must be in
> > ascending order, but key 'xxl' has value 38.5rem which isn't greater
> than
> > 38.5rem, the value of the previous key 'xl' !
> > >
> > > As there is nothing wrong with not growing the max-width at some
> point,
> > I
> > > don't think this should be warning. Checking that the values are
> > > monotonically increasing, but possibly unchanging should be enough to
> > > prevent accidental issues.
> > > Reduced test cases
> > >
> > > The following should be sufficient to reproduce the issue:
> > >
> > > $container-max-widths: (
> > > sm: 100%,
> > > md: 616px,
> > > lg: 616px,
> > > xl: 616px,
> > > xxl: 616px,
> > > );
> > > @import "bootstrap";
> > >
> > > What operating system(s) are you seeing the problem on?
> > >
> > > macOS
> > > What browser(s) are you seeing the problem on?
> > >
> > > Firefox
> > > What version of Bootstrap are you using?
> > >
> > > 5.3.8
> > >

author: CyrilKrylatov

body:
@CyrilKrylatov Thanks for checking this on both Firefox and Chrome. I appreciate the verification. After additional testing on my side, I’d like to clarify an important detail about the reproduction environment: This issue consistently occurs when the `` and matches the expected Bootstrap structure: ```
``` So while the markup appears identical to plain HTML after rendering, Bootstrap still applies standalone border-radius styling in this scenario. Interestingly: - the issue persists even when `.form-select` is explicitly applied via `CssClass` - the control renders as a direct sibling of `.input-group-text` - removing a framework-added styling class (`dg-single`) restores expected behavior This suggests Bootstrap may not always classify the rendered `

body:
Hi @CyrilKrylatov To answer your question directly: No, the issue does not reproduce in the provided CodePen example. In the plain HTML scenario there, the `.form-select` correctly merges with `.input-group-text` and the border-radius renders as expected. The behavior only appears in my actual application when using ASP.NET WebForms native server controls (asp:DropDownList), which I can't reproduce in CodePen due to limitations, even though ASP controls render as a standard: ```
``` So visually, the final DOM structure matches the Bootstrap example, but the rendered control still receives full border-radius styling instead of the clipped left edge expected inside an `.input-group`. Since the issue does not reproduce in the reduced plain HTML test case, I understand this likely means the behavior is caused by an interaction with framework-generated markup or additional styling present in the ASP.NET environment rather than Bootstrap itself. Thanks again for taking the time to check this.

> Description
>
> The current README.md provides a solid overview of Bootstrap 5, but as

> of Life" update to improve the developer onboarding experience.
> Proposed Improvements
>

>    - Task: Update the documentation setup instructions to include Bun
>    equivalents (e.g., bun install and bun run docs-serve) to maintain
>    consistency across the README.
>    -
>
>    *Roadmap Transparency (v6 Development):*
>    - The README mentions that the default branch is for "Bootstrap 5
>    release," but there is significant community interest in Bootstrap 6.
>    - Task: Add a small "Roadmap" or "Next Major Version" section (or a
>    link to a GitHub Project board) to clarify where the v6 development is
>    happening, similar to how the v4-dev branch is currently linked.
>    -
>

>       for a project of this scale.

author: coliff

body:
A problem that I had thought about a lot with using bootstrap-vue is that if you had a complete view of all of the components one used in their application, you could try to create a minimal set of the imported scss files. I never took it much further than just an idea. 

However, upon thinking about it in the modern day, with the limitation of static analyzers, an agent assisted pass could be most optimal in trying to guess for the smallest possible scss bundle size. It would be framework independent, and possibly be capable of inferring the finite class-set of something like: `const btnType = `btn-${variant}``

Again, this is something that human-intervention isn't usually worth it, instead a human is always going to optimize for their time here and simply import all of bootstrap. But an agent may be more capable of inferring than a simple static analysis which could lead to better performing applications. One would think that such a tool would have a larger impact than just for bootstrap, though. A true "holy grail" of css optimization is certainly a difficult task.

author: mdo

body: Chips are similar looking to badges and there is definitely overlap, but they still do have some different use cases. I think overall I think badges should be kept. If they were to be removed we'd want to provide a solution for this use case which is very useful:
author: coliff

body:
I'd keep both components in the library as their concepts are different:
- **Badge is a display primitive**. It represents system-assigned metadata: a status, a count, a label that the application sets. It has no interactive affordance and no form role. It lives contextually within or next to other content (inside a heading, anchored to a button, inline in a sentence). It scales with its parent font size. The user never creates or removes a badge; they just read it.
- **Chip input is a form control.** It's an interactive widget where the user types a value, confirms it (Enter or comma), and the value becomes a dismissible chip. It has a well-defined form submission contract (a hidden input collecting the values), focus management, keyboard navigation, and an explicit JS lifecycle. The user is the author of the chips, not a reader of them.

> [!NOTE]
> PrimeNg also have a [Badge](https://primeng.org/badge) and a [Chip](https://primeng.org/chip). We could look at how they are described.

Maybe our docs should anchor on intent, something like:
- "Use a badge when you want to communicate a system-determined value: a count, a status, a category label."
- "Use chip input when you want the user to enter and manage a collection of values as part of a form."

Ideally, visually, the 2 components could diverge more intentionnally to avoid misuse. If they look identical out of the box, developers will reach for the wrong one. I don't know how but the Chip could maybe communicate more about "I'm interactive".

Regarding the [Badge example page](https://v6-dev--twbs-bootstrap.netlify.app/docs/6.0/examples/badges/), I'd fold it into the Badge component page itself rather than keeping it as a standalone examples page. Examples section works best for page-level compositions that show how multiple components fit together (a marketing page, a dashboard shell, a sign-in form). A standalone "Badges" examples page doesn't meet that bar as badges aren't complex enough to warrant their own gallery IMHO, and the interesting patterns (notification counters, status pills in tables, label clouds) can all live as subsections in the component doc itself with a short "common patterns" block.

More precisely:
- The first 3 examples are already in the Badge page
- The Badge + avatar could be added to the Badge page
- The last 2 examples with the close button shouldn't be in the Badge page as it reinforces the misunderstanding between the usage of Badge and Chip, even if technically it's possible to do it.

body: That's all great feedback, and I hadn't seen Angular's badge/chip component comparison before. I had been thinking of doing something similar to make badges more of the super tiny inline counters a la PrimeNg, GitHub, etc. Might make that same distinction here too.

author: julien-deramond

body: v6-dev migrated off Popper to Floating UI, and the docs link to floating-ui.com rather than popper.js.org. Closing as resolved in v6-dev — please open a new issue if you spot a stale Popper link there.
author: mdo

body: Likely `scrollbar-gutter: stable` causing an issue? What version of Chrome and what version of Windows?
author: mdo

body:
Chrome: Version 149.0.7827.197 (Official Build) (64-bit)
Windows: Windows 11 IoT Enterprise LTSC Release: 24H2

I can confirm that `scrollbar-gutter: stable` is what's causing the issue. When I override it on `:root` and change it back to `auto`, everything works normally again.

Was this an intentional change to address a specific problem?

I feel that enabling this by default may not provide the best user experience, since it could force users to override this property in their own projects. Would it be possible to keep the previous Bootstrap v5 behavior instead?
author: ajiho

body:
@ajiho This to prevent scrollbars disappearing and causing a horizontal shift when freezing scrolling during things like an open dialog, open drawer, etc. It looks like Chromium browsers override user settings on Windows at an OS level—despite thin checkboxes not being checked, they still force it. With that Chromium flag enabled, there's no issue.

That said, I don't know what else to do here sadly. Will have to noodle on it more.
author: mdo

body:
A similar problem with dialogs and scrollbar-gutter stable started happening for me on Windows with an update to Chromium earlier this year.  For me including `clamp(100%, 40% + 0.666vw, 140%)` on my HTML size was causing the problem as it would adjust the text size when opening dialogs causing a layout shift on the page.

It only started happening with Chrome with the update as mentioned as I'd been using both for several years with no problem and had to readjust some designs pretty quickly to accommodate the change.

radwanalmsora

body: Fixed by https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/pull/42591
author: julien-deramond

body:
I'm OK with this approach. Adding coverage where it makes sense and ignoring file types where spellchecking isn’t useful sounds like the right balance.

If you have time, please go ahead and open a PR for v6 (it's easier to test compared to v5 with the local `npm run spellcheck` command). I'll backport the changes to v5 once it's merged.
